title: Drivers urged not to panic buy as fuel price hikes nowhere near record high
text:
Australians are being urged by a peak motorist association not to panic buy fuel amid a flare-up of tensions in the Middle East, saying prices are nowhere near April's record high.

Oil jumped to more than $US100 ($143) a barrel after renewed bombing began between Iran and the United States near vital shipping route the Strait of Hormuz.

Yemen's Houthi rebels this week struck two Saudi vessels in the Red Sea, a key route for Saudi Arabia to send its oil overseas since the strait was shut.

Commuters are now bracing themselves for the ripple effect at the petrol bowser.

NRMA spokesperson Peter Khoury said prices had already started to creep upwards.

NRMA is not forecasting prices to go as high as they were in April. (ABC News: Adam Griffiths)

"The average price for unleaded nationally has now gone up to $1.85. It's gone up just under 3 cents in the last 24 hours," Mr Khoury said.

"Diesel's gone up to $2.26 on average. It's gone up about 4 or 5 cents."

However, Mr Khoury said "there is a long way to go" before prices got to where they were a few months ago.

"To put that into context, when we hit the record highs in April, the average for unleaded was $2.50 … diesel was $3.27," he said.

" So based on those numbers, things would need to get pretty catastrophic to get back there again. "

'Profound impact'

Mr Khoury said price increases would have a "profound" effect, as significant price jumps or oil shocks had an "immediate inflationary impact on the economy".

Track the latest petrol and diesel prices around Australia See what's happening with petrol and diesel prices near you, updated constantly.

"It's not at the levels we saw in April, and we certainly hope we don't get anywhere near that, but what we do know is that petrol has a major impact on the family budget," he said.

"Every industry is impacted because our diesel economy runs on diesel; so farming and agriculture, mining, manufacturing, critically transport.

"That's why you see prices going up in the supermarket aisle as well as the bowser."

He said it was important to acknowledge the federal government's 16 cents per litre excise on wholesale fuel, halved from 32 cents per litre in July, was still in effect until August 2.

When asked whether the fuel excise should be extended, Mr Khoury said the government was keeping its options open and expected an announcement in coming days.

Warning against panic buying

Mr Khoury said the "most important" thing Australians could do was not panic buy fuel.

"One of the biggest challenges we faced as a country was at the start of the conflict when people began panic buying," he said.

"We saw Australians buying fuel at four and five times the rate they normally would and they weren't alone, we saw industry doing the same thing.

"It unnecessarily put pressure on our supply chain."

He said it also meant people who bought large amounts of petrol when prices were at a record high "ended up stockpiling fuel that they couldn't use and was too expensive" when prices promptly fell.

Mr Khoury suggested commuters and businesses research where they can get a good bargain.

Another hit to the pocket

Motorists in Sydney told the ABC on Friday morning the price jump would affect their movements.

An international student fuelling up her car in Sydney said the potential spike would coincide with her rent and lifestyle expense increases.

"I have my own car and I drive a lot, so yeah, I need to take public transport and it wastes my time and will also affect my study," she said.

Sydney motorists expressed some frustration at the rising price of fuel. (ABC News)

A manager of a logistics company said it would impact overhead costs, which "are going through the roof", as it uses a fleet of 20 to 30 cars.

"It puts the prices up and clients won't take your price, so they'll take someone that'll undercut you," he said.

" It means basically that you're not making enough money. You're going to lose jobs. "

A Tesla driver said his friends and family who drive petrol cars would feel the brunt, but he would still be affected.

"Things are getting very tight to be honest. I'm cutting back on quite a few things and focusing on essentials," he said.

title: Chalmers praises Rinehart for bonuses amid warning to not thank billionaire
text:
Freestyle king Kyle Chalmers has welcomed potential windfalls for Australia's Commonwealth Games swimmers after Gina Rinehart offered up cash bonuses for medals and world records.

Amid a spate of high-profile athlete withdrawals from Glasgow 2026, Dolphins squad members have been given a significant incentive to take part.

Billionaire Rinehart said she would deliver the same bonuses that were on offer in the pool at the 2024 Paris Olympics.

Individual swimmers will pocket $20,000 for a gold medal, $15,000 for silver and $10,000 for bronze and $30,000 for a world record.

Gina Rinehart and swimming legend Dawn Fraser watch on at the 2024 Australian Swimming Trials. (Getty Images: Quinn Rooney)

Relay team members will also be rewarded with $7,500 per person for a world record, $5,000 for a gold medal, $3,750 for silver and $2,500 for bronze.

Australians are well placed to cash in, with Canadian superstar Summer McIntosh skipping the Glasgow Games as she and other rivals focus on the ensuing Pan Pacific championships.

The swimming bonanza comes as many athletes in other sports lament a lack of financial support, despite Commonwealth Games Australia (CGA) providing a $5,000 grant to every member of the Glasgow team.

Glasgow was asleep and the Games weren't open as Australia got its first win Australia's first victory of the Commonwealth Games comes in a surprise extra session as the bowlers start before the opening ceremony.

"I'm super grateful to have the support of CGA to get us here, but to be able to have some medal incentive, it gives us a lot more purpose and reason," sprint star Chalmers said ahead of the opening ceremony.

"Otherwise you get a medal and the uniform ... so [I'm] very grateful to have supporters.

"CGA's made this possible, and the Commonwealth Games internationally being able to put it on, but to have some incentive for all of our hard work is crucial, especially for us older athletes."

The 28-year-old said Australia's swimmers were "very lucky" with the level of support they received compared to some other sports.

But the seven-time Commonwealth Games gold medallist said it remained "very challenging" for many swimmers to juggle arduous training schedules with work and study.

"It's a choice you make and I guess you kind of understand the financial position of the sport," Chalmers said.

"But fingers crossed there can be change for the next generation to kind of motivate them to continue to make the sacrifices and choose our sport, just to keep those talented athletes in there."

Petria Thomas has distanced the Commonwealth Games team from Ms Rinehart's bonuses. (Getty Images: Dean Mouhtaropoulos)

Rinehart's bonus scheme was announced by Swimming Queensland, of which the mining magnate is patron, while Australia's chef de mission Petria Thomas distanced CGA from it.

"That's a separate arrangement and it's not something that Commonwealth Games Australia is involved in whatsoever," former swimmer Thomas said.

"Good luck to the swimmers, I guess. They have someone backing that performance, but that's a separate matter for them."

A report in the Sydney Morning Herald on Friday said swimmers had been told not thank Rinehart when talking to the media after swimming.

The bonuses come on top of annual payments Rinehart makes to many of Australia's top-tier swimmers.

Athletes who win multiple medals will receive a payment for every medal they win.

Your guide to every day of the Comm Games in Australian time Here's the full schedule of every day of the 2026 Glasgow Commonwealth Games with all times and days in AEST.

In a statement, Rinehart said the bonuses were a way of Australia showing appreciation.

"There is something very uplifting about seeing an Australian athlete stand on the podium while our flag is raised," Rinehart said.

Meanwhile, Katarina Johnson-Thompson heads a list of nine members of the England athletics squad that pulled out of the Games on the morning of the opening ceremony.

Four more Olympic medallists – Dina Asher-Smith, Daryll Neita, Matthew Hudson-Smith and Amber Anning – are also out.

Like Keely Hodgkinson, the Olympic 800 metres champion who did not pursue selection, the injured and "gutted" Johnson-Thompson indicated she would switch her focus to the European championships in Birmingham next month.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP/ABC

title: Sam Kerr says A-League must find way to bring Matildas back, pay more
text:
A lot has been written and said about Sam Kerr over the years, but likening her to a Marvel superhero feels like a new comparison.

"I was really excited when I saw her coming on because I just had this Marvel moment where I was like, 'You have the Hulk," her teammate Midge Purce said after Kerr's influential cameo off the bench in her second game for her new club, which saw it come from 0-2 down to win 3-2.

"Like you thought this was everything and no, we're bringing on the Hulk."

A DC Comics-inspired reference might have been more apt considering she is now playing for Gotham FC, but whether she is Hulk, Batman, or any other fictional crime fighter, Kerr is already back lighting up the National Women's Soccer League (NWSL).

And she hasn't even scored yet.

Kerr made her Gotham FC debut last week. She used to play for the team under its previous banner, Sky Blue FC. (Getty Images: Dustin Satloff)

The Matildas captain opted to resume her career in the USA's top domestic league, after a hugely successful six and a half years with Chelsea in England's Women's Super League (WSL).

"I didn't really see myself starting fresh at a new European club. Coming back to the US has always been something that I wanted to do," Kerr told ABC Sport.

"Being close to [wife Kristie Mewis'] family and just coming back and hopefully winning some trophies.

"I felt like I'd achieved almost everything that I wanted to with Chelsea. So it was just time for change."

A-League Women needs more investment

When it became clear that Kerr was leaving Chelsea, there were some optimists hoping she might return home to Perth Glory in the A-League Women (ALW).

But that is incredibly unlikely while the league remains in its current semi-professional state.

Loading Instagram content

"We need to find a way to get all the best players back," Kerr said, urging greater investment in the Australian women's domestic game.

"If it paid enough and it was a top league, a lot of us Matildas would go back, but to better ourselves for the national team, we have to go overseas.

"I think the A-League was thriving, or the W-League back then was thriving when the national team players and players from the NWSL, players from England were coming over, and whether it was a guest stint or playing, you had the best players in the league, week in, week out, training with our future generation.

"I think the A-League's probably more similar to the NWSL. It's more transitional, probably on the same level as the NWSL off the pitch with things like that.

"But I think the A-League probably needs a little bit more investment, a little bit more time."

Kerr said most Matildas players have to go overseas to further their careers. (Getty Images: Paul Kane)

Kerr's thoughts reflect broader concerns from players, who have repeatedly spoken out about pay in the ALW, which is the lowest amongst Australia's major women's domestic competitions.

It was not so long ago that the NWSL and ALW, or W-League as it was known, were seen as two of the leading leagues in the world, as players split their time between both.

Now Kerr and goalkeeper Mackenzie Arnold are the only regular Matildas playing in the USA; a handful of others are based in Australia, with most opting to play in Europe.

American league growing

While the ALW has fallen well down the pecking order, the NWSL is now jostling for top billing alongside the WSL.

The NWSL was founded in 2013 with eight teams and now has 16 and has experienced significant commercial growth, exemplified when USA forward Trinity Rodman signed a three-year deal with Washington Spirit reportedly worth $2.86 million a year, making her one of the highest paid female footballers in the world.

Loading...

Gotham is the reigning NWSL champion, and in a huge next chapter, in 2028 will move from its base in New Jersey to a new purpose-built football stadium in Queens, shared alongside Major League Soccer's New York City FC.

Kerr's debut came off the bench in the team's win over Washington last week, which was played at the home of the New York Mets in front of more than 42,000 fans, a record for a women's sport event in New York City.

The home of Major League Baseball's New York Mets hosted Gotham FC's historic game, where Kerr made her club debut. (Getty Images: Dustin Satloff)

A few days later, her second game was a much more low-key affair, playing at a field enveloped by an athletics track, in front of several thousand fans who braved a 2 and a half hour weather delay.

For Kerr, 32, who had played for Gotham FC under its previous iteration as Sky Blue FC, the contrast in her first two games is emblematic of where the league is at.

"It's obviously changed a bit, but a lot has stayed the same too," she said.

"The fans and the stadiums that we're playing in now are what I think we should be playing in.

"And there's always been a lot of fun over here and a lot of excitement about the game, but in so many ways it still feels like the same league."

Kerr said the different styles of football in the NWSL and WSL make them difficult to compare.

Kerr has spent extended periods playing in the ALW, NWSL, and WSL. (Getty Images: Leila Coker/WSL)

"I think everyone's fighting for the top spot in the league, even in men's football, but I don't think it's worth putting my two cents in.

"I love both leagues, but yes, such different games in both countries."

Kerr's teammate, Ireland international Denise O'Sullivan, is another new acquisition for Gotham FC.

"I think it's the best league in the world; we've world-class players in every single team in this league," she said.

"And I think really the best thing about this league is the competitiveness from top to bottom. I don't think you can go into any game not right in it."

Kerr fandom high

Kerr's return is a major talking point for the NWSL and Gotham FC fans.

Amongst the crowd at her second game, there are already several fans sporting newly minted Kerr number 20 jerseys.

The Matildas captain is well known in the global women's football fanbase. (Getty Images: FIFA/Hassan Omar Wamwayi)

"Ecstatic! She's a blue; I'm a Chelsea fan, Gotham fan, so that collaboration, it just works together, I love it," Will from Jersey City said.

Teresa from Brooklyn said she was happy to see Kerr back with the New York-New Jersey-based club.

"Sam has been gone from the league for six and a half years, and we still see her name at the top of the NWSL scoring record. So if any NWSL fan doesn't know her, that's on them," she said.

There's even an eight-year-old girl named, what else but Matilda, or Tilly, who is there with her parents, having just moved to New York from Sydney.

"We love Sam Kerr and we're so excited that this is her team now, our home team is Sam Kerr's new team, super excited," her mum Kiki said.

Kerr is Australia's all-time leading goalscorer. (Getty Images: Paul Kane)

Not many Australians playing abroad attract this level of fandom, but even as she enters the latter stages of her career, Kerr still has the cachet amongst women's football fans here.

And she will be hoping her form can continue as she eyes off leading the Matildas to next year's Women's World Cup in Brazil.

title: TGA issues warning over rare eye disorder linked to Ozempic-like drugs
text:
The medicines regulator has issued a new safety warning for GLP-1-based drugs, like Ozempic, which could potentially cause a rare, but serious eye disorder.

The Therapeutic Goods Administration's (TGA) warning covers the following glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists which are used to treat type 2 diabetes and obesity:

Semaglutide, sold as Ozempic, Wegovy

Tirzepatide, sold as Mounjaro

Dulaglutide, sold as Trulicity

Liraglutide, sold as Saxenda

The TGA said the products had the potential to cause an eye disorder called non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION).

"… (NAION) may result in permanent visual impairment, including blindness, and no treatment has been shown to improve visual acuity outcomes," the TGA's safety warning stated.

NAION is caused by a disruption in blood flow to the optic nerve at the back of the eye and typically presents suddenly upon waking and without pain.

How Ozempic works in the brain to help people lose weight People on Ozempic and other GLP-1 agonist drugs commonly report they no longer have constant, incessant thoughts about food.

The regulator said anyone taking GLP-1 medicines should be alert to any signs of vision loss and stop taking the medicine and seek urgent medical attention if any changes were noted.

So far, 36 cases of the eye disorder associated with GLP-1 drugs, have been reported on the national database of adverse events, the TGA warned.

Twenty-three of those were among people taking semaglutide, three were taking liraglutide and 10 were using tirzepatide.

The TGA said it first identified the potential association between GLP-1 drugs and NAION in 2024 when a study was published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA) Ophthalmology.

The study examined almost 17,000 US patients and found those prescribed semaglutide had a higher risk of NAION than those taking non-GLP-1 medications for diabetes.

The TGA has issued a product warning for all GLP-1 RA medicines due to a risk of a rare, but severe form of eye disorder. (Flickr: Chemist 4 U)

The TGA said since this study it conducted its own independent assessment of the potential association between GLP-1 RAs and NAION and sought advice from the Advisory Committee on Medicines.

Optometry Australia, which represents the largest community of optometrists in Australia, said patients should not be unnecessarily alarmed by this update and do not need to stop taking a GLP-1 drug unless they had a change in their vision.

The professional body advised optometrists to ask patients presenting with sudden or unexplained vision loss whether they were currently using, or had recently used, a GLP-1 medicine.

Dr Flora Hui said about 70 per cent of people do not recover their vision if they develop the condition. (Supplied: Centre for Eye Research Australia)

Optometrist and vision scientist Dr Flora Hui from the Centre for Eye Research Australia said around 70 per cent of people don't recover their vision and someone's risk of developing NAION is also increased if they have diabetes, sleep apnea or hypertension.

People are also anatomically disposed to NAION if they have a crowded optic disc. The optic disc is where the optic nerve connects to the retina and when it is crowded nerve fibers are more vulnerable to blood supply problems.

Dr Hui said people should not panic about the TGA's updated advice.

"There is a small risk of developing NAION with these drugs so making informed choices are important, especially if you have many of the risk factors associated with NAION," she said.

"Reduce your other risk factors associated with NAION by improving heart health, blood pressure and cholesterol.

WHO endorses Ozempic-style drugs for weight loss Ozempic-style medications have been recommended as long-term treatments for obesity under new guidelines released by the World Health Organization.

"Most importantly have regular eye tests with your optometrist or ophthalmologist whilst you're being treated and letting them know that you're taking these medications."

Professor Garron Dodd, head of the Metabolic Neuroscience Laboratory, at the University of Melbourne thinks there's still a question mark over whether the GLP-1 based drugs actually cause NAION considering most people taking the drug already have known risk factors.

"Nearly all the evidence is retrospective, drawn from insurance claims and electronic health records … separating the drug from the condition it treats is the hard part, and no study has managed it cleanly."

He said it was important people did not stop taking their medication on the basis of this safety update.

"For someone with type 2 diabetes or significant obesity, the cardiovascular, kidney and metabolic benefits [of these medications] are large and well established, and stopping abruptly carries its own risks, so any change should be a conversation with your prescriber," he said.

Last June the World Health Organisation issued a warning about the risk of NAION from semaglutide medicines and The European Medicines Agency (EMA) recommended updating product information.

The EMA said NAION was a very rare side effect that potentially affected up to 1 in 10,000 users.

title: Four Corners withdraws episodes by sacked reporter
text:
The ABC has withdrawn the Four Corners programs Meth Highway and Cocaine Nation by sacked reporter Mahmood Fazal due to concerns about accuracy.

"This action has been taken following concerns identified about the veracity of certain sources used in both programs," a statement from ABC management said.

Fazal's employment was terminated after an ABC investigation into his appearance on a podcast with a former underworld figure last year, ABC managing director Hugh Marks confirmed at a Senate estimates hearing in May.

The ABC's statement said the decision to remove the Four Corners programs was separate to the investigation into Fazal's appearance on the podcast.

Mahmood Fazal no longer works at the ABC.

It said the matter had been referred to the ABC Ombudsman for an independent review of the programs' compliance with editorial standards.

"The ABC will consider whether any further action will be required once that review is complete," it said.

In Meth Highway, released in 2024, Fazal investigated the crystal meth pipeline from Melbourne to a major source of production in South-East Asia, the program said at the time.

Four Corners reporter Mahmood Fazal sacked by ABC Mahmood Fazal has been sacked by the ABC following an internal investigation into an appearance on a podcast hosted by a former underworld figure, managing director Hugh Marks says.

In 2023, Cocaine Nation covered the "shadowy underworld" of Australia's cocaine supply chain with "unprecedented access to the people involved in the trade", according to the episode's description.

It is not clear which parts of the episodes have been called into question.

The withdrawal comes at a turbulent time for the broadcaster after the sudden departure of news director Justin Stevens in May.

Mr Marks confirmed he and Stevens disagreed about the direction of the organisation and announced a replacement, Reuters executive editor Simon Robinson, the following day.

Mahmood Fazal was an investigative reporter for ABC RN's Background Briefing before his Four Corners role. (ABC)

Fazal, a former member of the Mongols bikie club, is a Walkley Award–winning journalist specialising in crime, violence, imprisonment and terrorism.

Before working at Four Corners, he was an investigative reporter for ABC RN's Background Briefing.

Prior to joining the ABC, he worked for VICE as a senior staff writer and host of the series Violent Times.

His reporting has appeared in The Guardian, Rolling Stone, The Sydney Morning Herald, The Monthly and The Saturday Paper.

Fazal used his contacts to line up crime stories and investigations, including with gang members.

He has been contacted for comment.

Joel Tozer, who previously led 7.30, was appointed executive producer of Four Corners in December 2025.

As one of his first management changes, he advertised for the role of an editor, commissioning and standards, who would be charged with "leading commissioning and standards to maintain the quality and integrity of the ABC's flagship investigative journalism program".

title: Norway set to file complaint over Trump's push to overturn red card
text:
US President Donald Trump's role in clearing Folarin Balogun from a mandatory one-game ban at the World Cup is set to provoke a formal complaint to FIFA ethics investigators.

The Norway soccer federation said its board of directors will consider filing the complaint at its next meeting, likely on August 6.

"We need our leader in FIFA to say that this was an error," Norwegian federation president Lise Klaveness told The Times in an interview.

After Trump called FIFA president Gianni Infantino, a one-game ban for Balogun's red-card tackle against Bosnia-Herzegovina was deferred so he could play against Belgium with a quarterfinals place at stake.

Belgium beat the US 4-1 after a chaotic 36 hours at the World Cup. The Belgium players were seemingly fired up by one of the greatest controversies in tournament history.

FIFA president Gianni Infantino (left) with Crown Prince Haakon of Norway and Football Association of Norway president Lise Klaveness. (Getty Images: Jean Catuffe)

Trump called the referee's red card for Balogun after video review a "horrible" call and took credit for getting FIFA to review what should routinely have been a one-game ban.

"We all know that this verdict was influenced from external forces and did not have the proper process," Klaveness said of the FIFA ruling that was unprecedented in the past 60 years at World Cups.

"When you bend a rule like this, you're on a slippery slope that will put the whole game at risk," said the former Women's World Cup player for Norway, who is an elected member of European soccer body UEFA's executive committee.

Klaveness is a lawyer and judge who has positioned the Norwegian federation as a conscience in world soccer since her election in 2022.

"It is a worry for the game when you compromise fundamental rules of the game," she said about not enforcing Balogun's ban.

Norway previously joined a London-based NGO's complaint to the FIFA ethics committee alleging Infantino breached a statutory duty of political neutrality, including by creating a peace prize to award to Trump at the World Cup draw in December.

EU politicians launch bid to investigate FIFA boss A group of European politicians launches a bid to begin an investigation into how and why a red card for an American football star was overturned at the World Cup.

The close personal and political ties between Trump and Infantino include the US president believing the soccer official could be a good pick as next secretary general of the United Nations, the New York Post reported this week.

The human rights NGO, FairSquare, also has filed an ethics complaint about the Balogun incident to the International Olympic Committee, where Infantino is an elected member, about breaching its rules on political neutrality.

FIFA and the IOC rarely acknowledge or provide updates on potential cases before their ethics bodies.

The independence of FIFA's process to handle complaints alleging unethical conduct has been questioned since the lead investigator and judge both were replaced in 2017, in the second year of Infantino's presidency.

FIFA has yet to publish documents explaining how its disciplinary judges reached verdicts about Balogun or other World Cup players.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AP

title: MotoGP to rev up motorcycle culture and pilgrimages to City of Churches
text:
The arrival of the Tour Down Under to Adelaide in 1999 inspired a significant uptick in Lycra-wearing cyclists in and around Adelaide, a trend that continued well into the late 2010s.

But with MotoGP landing in the City of Churches from 2027, and a Superbike World Championship round in Tailem Bend from 2028, it poses the question: Will this result in thousands of high-performance sport bikes whizzing around our roads?

Noah Orozgani works in sales at family business Moto Adelaide and says retailers believe the MotoGP is a major coup for the state.

"Like the time we had a big name like [MotoGP rider] Jack Miller come and ride for the Australian Superbikes," he says.

"There was big crowd there at Tailem Bend, the racetrack and people just went there to watch Jack Miller.

" Now there will be 20-something potential world champions riding through the streets of Adelaide. "

He says the MotoGP bikes and their riders are "out of this world" in terms of their performance and the excitement would rub off on spectators of all ages, which would in turn be good for dealerships and service centres.

Popular Australian MotoGP rider Jack Miller was rolled out for the SA government's February announcement. (ABC News: Che Chorley)

"The fan base is not like anything else. They get really riled up, like footy fans," he says.

"People will fly in to be a part of it, it will be huge, and getting to watch them for real, sales will go berserk.

"People are doing 360 kilometres per hour, and it just gives you that buzz, and they'll go: 'Oh yeah, I've got a bike at home, I'll go riding, and then the bike's not good enough, I need an upgrade, or a service.'"

Revving up hype

Motorcycle Alliance of South Australia chairman Heath Norris says it's likely a lot more motorcycles will be brought out of people's sheds.

"It always brings a bit of hype along with it, this sort of thing," he says.

He says Adelaide already has a strong motorcycle culture, with "great roads, great scenery and great destinations", as well as flagship charity events, like the Motorcycle Riders' Association of SA Toy Run.

Heath Norris during the 2025 charity toy run. (Supplied: Heath Norris)

That being said, he points out there is a difference between the high-performance sport bikes, or "crotch rockets" developed into superbikes, and cruiser bikes like Harley-Davidsons.

"But we're all bike enthusiasts at the end of the day," Mr Norris says.

The South Australian government is banking on strong interest in the MotoGP to warrant expenditure forecast by the former F1 Grand Prix track designer to be more than $100m to adapt the racecourse alone.

It is also facing strong and ongoing public criticism over the removal of Parklands trees that will be required to adjust the track for the MotoGP.

Banking on a big impact

A spokesperson says the 2025 MotoGP event at Phillip Island delivered an average of "$217 million in economic impact for local economies through "global exposure combined with tourism and hospitality".

Estimates MotoGP remodel could cost $100m Adelaide Street Circuit designer Bob Barnard estimates it could cost more than $100 million to remodel the track for the MotoGP.

"MotoGP has a global fan base of more than 639 million people, with a record 3.6 million attendees in races in 2025 and TV audiences up 9 per cent over the past year," he says.

The spokesperson says it is a "major global sporting property with an established national and international audience".

"South Australians will no doubt embrace motorcycle racing with the same enthusiasm we've embraced cycling, Supercars, golf and the arts, among many others."

A world first

While Victoria's Phillip Island Grand Prix racecourse is regarded as among the best worldwide, enthusiasts have said the location had limits when it came to accessibility.

The Adelaide MotoGP street circuit will be in the city's east parklands and Victoria Park. (ABC News: Daniel Taylor)

A street circuit like Adelaide, the world's first, was expected to draw significant international visitors, and its timing alongside the Supercars Championship Adelaide Grand Final would further bolster attention.

Mr Orozgani points out that every year there was a huge contingent of people who would ride bikes over to Phillip Island via the Great Ocean Road to watch the MotoGP.

"Religiously, they went to Melbourne to watch the races and come back," Mr Orozgani says.

Describing the end of that annual event as a "downside" to the MotoGP coming to SA, he adds it may mean people interstate will ride their bikes to Adelaide instead.

The Phillip Island GP track is considered among the best of its kind in the world. (Supplied: Ading Attamimi)

Raising awareness

Mr Norris hopes an increased profile through motorcycle racing will improve road awareness among other road users, pointing out that Adelaide drivers are not always willing to share the road.

Some Adelaide drivers struggle to accommodate road cyclists as well, particularly those who legally ride two abreast. (891 ABC Adelaide: Malcolm Sutton)

"I've ridden in Queensland, and people get out of your way when they see you coming up behind," he says.

" It's just that some people are quite arrogant here. They think that lane filtering is illegal, for example. "

Lane filtering is when a motorcycle rider edges between two lanes of stationary traffic on the same side of the road, or traffic moving at 30km/h or less in the same direction, which is a legal and practical activity to ease congestion.

"A lot of people here are oblivious to motorcycles," Mr Norris says.

Dealerships are expecting the MotoGP to boost Adelaide interest in motorcycles. (891 ABC Adelaide: Malcolm Sutton)

Asked if SAPOL was expecting more high-performance sport bikes on the roads as a result of the MotoGP, Officer in Charge of Traffic Services Branch Superintendent Shane Johns says it is committed to improving road safety for all road users.

"Major events can result in increased traffic volumes, including more motorcycles on our roads," he says.

"SAPOL routinely plans for these events with an appropriate policing response, which includes targeted road safety operations where required.

"SAPOL encourages all motorcyclists to ride within the speed limit, to the conditions, and to make responsible decisions on the road."

A motorcyclist caught at 200 kilometres per hour in an 80km/h zone in the Adelaide Hills during 2015. (Supplied: SA Police)

title: Fishing ban considered as NSW South Coast abalone populations depleted
text:
Once the New South Wales South Coast was brimming with abalone, but today most of the populations are depleted and the state government is considering a ban on commercial fishing to allow the shellfish to repopulate.

In a draft management proposal seen by the ABC, the Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development (DPIRD) has proposed a shutdown of commercial abalone harvesting from Bundeena, south of Sydney, to Eden near the Victorian border.

The proposal cites pressure from overfishing and "resource-sharing issues" with native title holders as creating "significant sustainability challenges".

The proposal would result in about 350 kilometres of coastline being declared off-limits for commercial abalone fishers.

Abalone Association of NSW (AANSW) chair Stephen Bunney said the mood among divers was one of dismay.

"It has now got to a state where three-quarters of the fishery is probably unrecoverable," he said.

" It's certainly no longer commercially viable. "

Under the proposal, the Central Zone between Bundeena and Red Point would be closed to commercial abalone harvesting. (Supplied)

In a statement, a DPIRD spokesperson said the draft was based on a reduction in commercial abalone harvest to relieve pressure on the resource and submissions from stakeholders were being considered.

Fishing quota slashed

The latest report from the independent Total Allowable Fishing Committee (TAFC), a statutory body that reviews fishing catch limits, found there was a "severe decline" in abalone stocks across the NSW coastline in the past year to either depleting or depleted.

In response, DPIRD last month slashed the amount of abalone able to be caught in the upcoming season by more than 40 per cent.

A major issue identified by the TAFC was illegal, unreported or unregulated (IUU) fishing, which the DPIRD has previously estimated at between 25 and 35 tonnes of abalone each year.

Stephen Bunney says the fishery has "pretty much collapsed" north of Eden. (Supplied)

Mr Bunney said the rezoning and closure would not address their ongoing concerns about the government's inability to stop IUU fishing.

"Many people have just lost absolute confidence in the government to manage this," he said.

" The only thing they can do, if they're not going to pursue compliance and illegal fishing, is remove the commercial fishery because it's the only thing they've got control over. "

Abalone stocks across the NSW South Coast are depleting or depleted. (ABC South East NSW: Vanessa Milton)

Native title holders 'used as a scapegoat'

The stretch of coastline that would be closed is subject to an ongoing native title claim filed by the South Coast people in 2017.

While the native title applicants have welcomed the proposed closure, applicant and long-time advocate for Aboriginal cultural fishing rights, Wally Stewart, said the South Coast Aboriginal community was being "used as a scapegoat" for the depletion.

Wally Stewart is a lead applicant in the South Coast people's native title claim. (ABC South East NSW: Vanessa Milton)

Under Commonwealth native title law passed in 1993, Aboriginal cultural fishers are entitled to take sea resources under traditional lore and custom without restrictions under state fisheries management laws.

But Mr Stewart said it had been a protracted battle for the state government to respect the rights.

"We always knew that it was our water and our resources, and there was even a law there to back it up," he said.

" But the NSW government discriminated against that and prosecuted our people under state laws. "

The DPIRD and the TAFC includes the legally protected native title catch within the IUU take.

But native title applicants say that classification has fuelled discrimination.

Abalone is a traditional food for South Coast Indigenous people. (ABC South East NSW: Vanessa Milton)

"They've made us out to be poachers and the ones that destroyed the fishery," Mr Stewart said.

"We'd be lucky to take 2 tonnes a year between the whole mob up and down the South Coast.

"In the meantime, the commercial divers have been taking around 100 tonnes a year."

The continued prosecution of native title holders by the state government under NSW fisheries management regulations was the subject of a 2022 parliamentary inquiry and a class action that was filed in 2024.

Buyout offer rejected

The draft management framework is part of the state government's attempt to reform and reset the abalone fishery that was announced in June.

It included offering a voluntary share buyout to commercial abalone fishers at a valuation of about $4,200 a share to reduce fishing pressure.

There are currently 41 shareholders in the industry with shareholdings between 10 and 90 shares, meaning the largest shareholders would receive $378,000 under the offer.

But on July 10, the AANSW voted to reject the government's offer.

Mr Bunney said the offer was unfair and poorly formulated in light of what the industry believed was a failure to police illegal fishing.

"They've offered them less than a third of what they've paid to be in these businesses when the fishery was being properly managed," he said.

"This is people's businesses. This is people's lives."

The commercial abalone industry has rejected the government's offer of a voluntary share buyout. (ABC South East NSW: Vanessa Milton)

But Mr Stewart said the government should be focused on habitat recovery rather than compensation.

"That money should go into restoration, rather than giving these guys a golden handshake," he said.

Mr Bunney said AANSW would be asking for mediation with Agriculture Minister Tara Moriarty while also pushing for an upper house inquiry.

title: Why Waratahs 'trust each other' to deliver in grand final against Drua
text:
Confidence can sometimes be mistaken for arrogance, but NSW Waratahs captain Emily Chancellor knows how to distinguish between the two.

Therefore, it is no surprise the Waratahs took pride in their 33-22 win over the Western Force in the Super Rugby Women's semifinals, but refused to get carried away with their performance.

"We got out on the field in that semifinal and there was just this air of clarity around the group," Chancellor told ABC Sport.

"It wasn't arrogance. It was just this feeling of we know what to do and we know how to do it, so let's just go out and execute."

As they prepare to face Fijian Drua in Saturday's grand final in Ba, Fiji, the Waratahs have reason to be in a confident mood.

Last Saturday against the Force in Perth, the defending champions produced arguably the best 40 minutes of rugby from any side this season.

They ran in four tries during the first half and led 26-0 before the Force were able to score their first points.

The Force, who earned hosting rights by finishing above the Waratahs on the standings, clawed back some ground on the scoreboard in the second half.

But the result was never in doubt.

"We know we've got firepower out wide. We know we've got electric backs," Chancellor said.

"We've got a really strong, physical forward pack that has been quite consistent across the last few years.

"We know the forwards are doing a really good job of going direct and then it's up to the backs to do what they do best."

Waiaria Ellis was superb for the Waratahs in their semifinal win over the Force. (Getty Images: Janelle St Pierre)

It was a return to form for the Waratahs, who entered the play-offs in third place.

They dropped their final two regular-season matches against the Force (28-21) and the Queensland Reds (26-19).

The loss to the Force was the first time they had been defeated by an Australian opponent in Super Rugby Women's history.

But sudden-death rugby has become second nature to the Waratahs, who have only missed one grand final among the seven contested in the competition.

The six-time winners were rewarded for taking a punt on an untried halves pairing, Tia Hinds and Waiaria Ellis, with the selection move proving to be a masterstroke.

Hinds, an Australian sevens star making her Waratahs debut, was a revelation at scrum half, scoring two tries when she caught the Force's ruck defence napping.

She combined brilliantly with 18-year-old Wallaroos sensation Ellis, who helped drive the Waratahs' attack in her first start at fly half this season after switching from fullback.

"They (Hinds and Ellis) backed themselves," said Chancellor, who has played almost 40 Tests for the Wallaroos.

"Tia, being an incredible reader of space, took her own opportunities, and Baba (Ellis) did a really good job of being calm and collected.

"She had a really sound knowledge of our game plan and was just able to deliver and execute what she saw in front of her."

Tahs face stern test

The Drua, who have won two Super Rugby Women's championships, were impressive in their 31-5 victory over the Reds in the first semifinal in Lautoka last Saturday.

They deservedly finished the regular season on top of the standings with a 3-1 win-loss record.

But their one defeat came at the hands of the Waratahs, who triumphed 42-29 at Leichhardt Oval in the opening round.

The Waratahs defeated the Drua when they played in the opening round of the regular season. (Getty Images: Mark Metcalfe)

"Fiji would probably be disappointed that it's us in the final," Chancellor said.

"We're probably the hardest ones for them to beat, in the same way they're the hardest ones for us to beat. They've been playing some really great rugby.

"They play a style that is hard to defend against, but in the same breath it creates a lot of opportunity because they do play a high-risk, high-reward version of rugby.

"So if we can withstand some pressure and stay disciplined, I believe we have an attacking game that will be hard to contain."

Chancellor's faith in her Waratahs teammates is not misplaced, as it is built on a key trait the entire squad shares: confidence.

"We're a squad who knows how to play big games," she said.

"The Waratahs are a team that knows how to win in big moments. We know how to win; we know how to perform under these pressure moments.

"We take a lot of confidence because we trust each other to do our own jobs."

title: Members of a 'surreal' rescue off WA's Stirling Range reunite
text:
Lucy Fairweather was hiking the West Australia's Stirling Range Ridge Walk in April of this year, taking in stunning views as she ate her lunch.

Unfortunately, she broke her ankle shortly after and had to be winched off the mountain pass by helicopter.

The mountainous, multi-day hike across the Stirling Range National Park is considered one of the state's toughest.

Her rescue marked the 11,000th mission for the RAC rescue helicopter service since it was established in 2003.

Ms Fairweather said the rescue was surreal.

"To be rescued off the mountain within three hours of having actually fallen was way faster than I expected," she said.

"I think just as you get lifted off the ground and feel your weight being taken by that bag, and then you start to see the view, where you were receding — crazy experience."

Thankfully, the Perth woman is nearly off her crutches and was reunited with the helicopter crew to celebrate the milestone earlier this week.

Bluff Knoll, in the Great Southern, is one of WA's highest peaks. (ABC News: Andrew Chounding)

'Amazing' to see patients smiling

St John Ambulance critical care paramedic James Vine said it has been amazing to reconnect with Ms Fairweather.

"It's always wonderful to touch base with patients again, and see the actual recovery and close that loop really for us," he said.

"We get kind of a real small snapshot of patients and their injuries and their families, but to see a smiling face rather than a grimacing face is always nice."

A critical care paramedic winches a patient aboard a rescue helicopter alongside Stirling Range Ridge Walk. (Supplied: DFES)

Mr Vine said it was not often you attend the scene of a rescue and people have the right gear.

"You often get down there and find that people aren't as well prepared as maybe they should be," he said.

"It was a refreshing change for ourselves; they were in the middle of a multi-day hike, so they were well prepared.

It has been an above-average year for rescues from the Stirling Range, with six already recorded.

Lucy Fairweather had to be rescued via helicopter when she injured her foot while hiking the Stirling Range Ridge Walk. (ABC News: Jon Daly)

'We were so impressed'

Ms Fairweather said it was great to come back and see her rescuers.

"It was really cool to get the chance to come back and say hi and hear about it from their perspective," she said.

"Particularly, they said at the time that it was the 11,000th rescue."

A rescue team prepares to winch down to a stranded hiker. (Supplied: DFES)

Conducting a rescue is not a simple task: with about 25 people from different emergency services involved, it takes about eight hours from start to finish.

RAC Rescue helicopter pilot Matthew Mayne said Ms Fairweather's rescue was more efficient than usual.

"The Stirling Ranges is a challenging, difficult environment, but the weather always has the last say," he said.

"We flew over the ridge line where Lucy said she was, and she was exactly where she said, and the cloud was above us, the winds were light.

"Probably not even 10 minutes later, we came back in to extract both [Lucy and the paramedic] off the ridge line."

A RAC Rescue helicopter flying past Ellen Peak in WA's Stirling National Park. (Supplied: DFES)

Three months after Ms Fairweather was told by the doctor she had "shattered" her ankle, she is looking to make a full recovery.

title: Budjerah's debut album Gentleman has been a long time coming
text:
For the past four years, Coodjinburra singer/songwriter Budjerah has been patient.

The two-time ARIA Award-winning music artist has just released his debut album, Gentleman.

But it took a while to get there.

"I think even by music industry norms, four years is a long time to get that many songs together," Budjerah said.

"I think I've written maybe three albums in the meantime separate from just these 11 tracks, and it started in 2022; I'd won two ARIAs and I really wanted to make an album.

"I didn't feel creatively satisfied, just having these little short bursts of creativity with the EPs."

Budjerah, 24, spent a month writing "a tonne of songs" with Australian singer/songwriter Matt Corby for what would have been his debut album, but just one of those songs, titled Video Game, was released, in July 2023.

Platinum and 2x ARIA Award-winning music artist Budjerah has released his debut album Gentleman. (Supplied: Daniel Boud)

In February and March of that year, Budjerah went on tour with Ed Sheeran as the support act for the Australian leg of Sheeran's Mathematics tour.

This coincided with the release of Budjerah's hit single Therapy in February 2023.

"I think the success of Therapy at the beginning of 2023, people just wanted to hear that one song," Budjerah says.

"So, I was on tour, and I was doing two to three shows a week for a year, and then more after that, and I didn't get the time to actually finish the songs, or work on any more songs, because it was all go, go, go, go, go, we need to boost this one song."

A true Gentleman

Gentleman is produced by the Grammy Award-winner Om'Mas Keith (Frank Ocean, Jay-Z), and musically it is inspired by the songs Budjerah grew up listening to.

Gentleman is out now. (Supplied: Warner Music)

The 11 tracks have a timeless R&B/pop sound including the first single, Want You Back, which quickly caught the attention of Elton John.

"I just got a call one day, and they said listen in from here, they're going to play your song on his radio show, Rocket Hour on Apple," Budjerah said.

"And so, I stayed up till the middle of the night to listen to it."

It was a full circle (of life) moment given The Lion King is Budjerah's favourite movie.

The funky, high-energy title track takes its cue from the likes of James Brown, who Budjerah is a big fan of via his uncles and grandfather.

"I felt like it was a fun way to play up the performance aspect of what I do, because everything I do is about the performance," Budjerah says.

"When I'm in the studio, I think about how the song is going to be interpreted live."

Budjerah loves to perform live so much, he even sang at his own wedding to high school sweetheart Sophie Slabb (née Moore), according to Vogue Australia.

Budjerah married his high school sweetheart, Sophie. (Supplied: Elisha Lindsay and Ashi Hilmer courtesy of Vogue Australia)

"I'm lucky I've been able to experience love," Budjerah told the ABC.

"I don't sing nothing that I don't mean.

"I always relate it to my personal experience and my stories."

The catchy Could I Be is exactly what he's talking about.

Budjerah is going on tour to support his new album.

His official tour will kick off in Melbourne on October 31, then he'll head to Anglesea in Victoria, Sydney, and the Gold Coast, before wrapping up in Brisbane on Saturday, November 21.

Budjerah collaborated with a host of esteemed songwriters on Gentleman including the legendary Diane Warren (Celine Dion and Beyoncé) on Even at My Worst.

"She's one of my songwriting heroes," he says.

"And so, getting to meet her, and then getting to sing one of her songs was just a once-in-a-lifetime experience for me."

There were some tracks on the album, however, that felt counterintuitive to Budjerah, who was raised in church to parents who were both musicians — but also pastors.

Those songs are Hard Time and Sinner.

"I thought it was funny, being a little gospel singer from Fingal Head given the song called Sinner," Budjerah said.

"And then a song called Hard Time, a lot of Aboriginal people do a bit of a hard time.

"So, I thought it was a bit of an odd topic, but my dad said, 'do both if you have enough time'.

"My dad just said, 'those two songs are perfect'.

"They weren't the obvious picks for me, but we just had fun with them, and I really enjoyed singing both of them. They fit really well for my voice."

Keeping the faith

Speaking to ABC News from the house he grew up in, Budjerah spun around and held up his Bible when asked if he still carried one around.

"I always keep it with me," he said.

"Usually, some hotels and motels have a Bible in the drawer, but not all the time, and sometimes it's just something that I need.

"So, I carry it with me, and I read it, and my faith has been something that's been really helpful and important to me, especially when I'm on the road and travelling so much, it keeps me together."

Born Budjerah Julum Slabb, the artist is from the small coastal village of Fingal Head in the far north-eastern corner of NSW.

"I had a very fun childhood," he says.

"I enjoyed the music, a very loving family, and some people have a hard time, like, other pastor's kids that I know, but I've been very blessed that I've had super supportive parents and family.

"I got to be a kid, I got to listen to good music, I got to go surfing."

Budjerah says he wants to be a "triple threat". (Supplied: Daniel Boud)

You can still find Budjerah, the eldest of four, in church on a Sunday.

"My grandfather, he goes to a church across the river that I go to, and I try to sneak in, but … I end up singing a song almost every time.

" So, if anyone wants a free concert, you might see me in a church on a Sunday, any old Sunday, I might be singing. "

Finding his voice

Budjerah first burst out on the scene as a fresh-faced teenager on The Voice, but not a single judge (Delta Goodrem, Guy Sebastian, Boy George, Kelly Rowland) turned their chair, despite a standing ovation from the audience.

"It was a pretty wild experience, especially for a 16-year-old," he says.

"I think that that was the first time that I truly experienced heartbreak, as a young person.

"I felt like my whole dream was just crushed.

"But [I] always remember Kelly Rowland just telling me, just keep singing, just keep singing.

"And it'll work out. When it's meant to happen, it'll happen."

It's not the only audition he's failed to land.

Budjerah says he tried out for the role of Malakai in the Netflix series Heartbreak High.

"Thomas Weatherall got the role. I didn't get the role," Budjerah says.

"So, I think it's really cool that I still get to be in the show somehow, with my music in there [on the soundtrack]."

He still has ambitions to act.

"I want to be a triple threat, so singing, dancing, acting," he says.

"I write songs as well, so, I guess my personal dream is to be the ultimate entertainer, so I spend a lot of time practising my dances and singing every day, and dancing every day, and acting.

"What got me into performing was doing the school musicals and school plays.

"So, I always thought maybe one day I'd come back to acting."

Gentleman is out now and Budjerah's official album tour will kick off in Melbourne in October.

title: Spinal injury brings unusual hospital stay with hen's party and wedding
text:
Christine Olsen-Lane was overcome with emotion when she finally left Sunshine Coast Health's rehabilitation unit this week.

Staff and patients lined the hallways to bid her farewell.

Loading...

"Every one of you has contributed to my journey, and all I can say is thank you," the 71-year-old said through tears.

"But that is a big, big, big thank you … I love you all."

Her send-off celebration came after a whirlwind 12 months that included a life-threatening fall, multiple operations and intense rehabilitation — not to mention a proposal, hen's party and a wedding.

The couple married at Caloundra Powerboat Club with more than 100 guests in attendance. (Supplied)

The fall

Ms Olsen-Lane and her partner Peter Lane had been working in the garden of their Tin Can Bay home in August last year when she tripped on chicken wire.

"I fell, hit my head on the corner of the plinth of the hot water service that's outside and aggravated an old neck injury that I'd had from 50 years ago when I dived into a swimming pool," she said.

" I certainly did a job on myself. "

Christine Olsen-Lane with some of the "miracle workers" who helped her to get back on her feet. (Supplied: Sunshine Coast Health)

She had damaged her third, fourth and fifth vertebrae and was rushed to Sunshine Coast University Hospital's intensive care unit in a "bad way".

"I could not move my hands or my feet. I was fed by a tube for 12 weeks," she said.

" All I could do was put a little sponge in my mouth that I could push up to the roof of my mouth and let the water dribble down my throat because I couldn't swallow. "

Hospital staff helped Christine Olsen-Lane prepare for her wedding, even decorating her wheelchair. (Supplied)

Mr Lane, who had only just proposed to Ms Olsen-Lane, was beside himself.

"It was pretty scary, pretty intense," he said.

"I thought she was going to be a quadriplegic for the rest of her life."

But with the help of doctors and the rehabilitation team, who the couple described as "miracle workers", she was able to stand and has recently started taking steps again.

"The brightness of the staff and everyone just lifted her, it was unbelievable," Mr Lane said.

"Roosters" were also welcome at the hen's party, where everyone dressed up and ate party food. (Supplied)

Why wait?

Mr Lane was by his partner's bedside every day during her recovery, staying at Wishlist House, which provides affordable accommodation for patients' loved ones.

"We muddled along so much, he said to me, 'Let's still go ahead and get married'," Ms Olsen-Lane said.

"We had a hen's party at the hospital, and a lot of the people in wheelchairs and those getting treatment all came.

"We had a hen's and an old rooster's party … all the staff came, the doctors, the nurses, the wardsmen. I've made so many friends."

Loading...

That almost became a problem ahead of the big day.

"I was going to have Pete and my granddaughters as bridesmaids, and my sister and then the friends from Tin Can Bay said, 'We want to be bridesmaids.'

"Then the nursing staff said, 'We want to be bridesmaids.' Dr Neala [Milburn], who is the head spinal doctor, said, 'I want to be a bridesmaid.'

" I ended up having 15 bridesmaids. We had the happiest wedding and you can feel the love in the room. "

The happy couple celebrated their big day with family, friends and hospital staff. (Supplied)

The next steps

They even had a honeymoon, of sorts.

"There's an independent living unit [at the hospital] that they said that we could have for the night," Ms Olsen-Lane said.

"They had set it up with party lights, streamers, balloons. Even the patients had cut out hearts strewn over the beds that were in there for us.

"Everybody had just gone to so much trouble, and it was lovely."

The couple stayed in a "honeymoon suite" at the hospital on their wedding night, decorated by staff and patients. (Supplied)

Sunshine Coast Health clinical coach for rehabilitation Taylor James said she thought Ms Olsen-Lane would end up in aged care when she first arrived.

“She was very dependent on us for all of her care, but the fact that she is standing now and able to have a really good quality of life [shows] she's worked really hard," Ms James said.

"It's really helped a lot of our other patients. Seeing how many have come out to her [farewell] party shows how much of an impact she's made on our ward — I'm going to really miss her."

Ms Olsen-Lane was a blushing bride on her wedding day. (Supplied)

The newlywed will continue her rehabilitation at home with the help of her devoted husband.

title: Out of necessity is borne a unique and wholly Scottish opening ceremony
text:
The Glasgow Commonwealth Games was more or less forced inside.

The shortened preparation time meant it was simply easier to host most events in halls and exhibition centres that already existed, and a solid roof removes the many variables that Scotland's weather can introduce to proceedings.

It meant the opening ceremony, for the first time in history, would be held in an arena rather than a stadium. And what a blessing it turned out to be.

Australia emerged late in the athletes' parade, which was divided by regions as well as nations. (Getty Images: James Fearn)

If you've ever witnessed a rock band or pop star playing before a major football game you'll know the energy is very rarely on point.

It's just too far, the noise dissipates, and charismatic frontmen and global superstars are reduced to little more than carnival barkers, pleading with a distracted crowd to "put your hands up. Please".

On Thursday night in Glasgow the crowd filed in as the perfect weather turned cool for the first time this week. Local DJ Hu Sane and radio presenters kept the crowd relatively entertained with the sort of pre-show antics you'd expect from a warm-up act.

The opening skit with cycling great Sir Chris Hoy, former Doctor Who star Sylvester McCoy and actor Greg McHugh served its purpose of delivering a few knowing chuckles and delivering King Charles III and Queen Camilla in a TARDIS.

King Charles III's arrival was the sort of thing we've come to expect from opening ceremonies. The rest of the show wasn't. (AP: Manish Swarup)

But from the opening strains of synth-heavy electro-folk band Valtos it was clear this ceremony would feel different.

As the athletes entered the arena, messages from local kids or well-meaning and poorly read adults, were projected on the screens wrapping around the upper decks.

"I hope you win the game, good luck best wishes from Gulalai" "Good luck! I hope you enjoy Glasgow — our green place, you can do this!" "Have fun you can do it. I hope you do well be brave." "Good luck. Believe in yourself be proud of yourself. From Leah."

Nina Kennedy and Tim Hodge led the team into the arena. (Getty Images: Michael Steele)

Scotland is a nation that prides itself on being small but mighty. Fittingly, the smaller the team the larger the cheer during the parade, with the loudest reserved for St Lucia.

The tiny Caribbean nation brought just three athletes to Glasgow and only the chef de mission marched. But she was welcomed like a Glaswegian local.

Then came the main attraction.

When in doubt, pipe. (Getty Images: Mattia Ozbot)

Scotland's athletes danced their way to their seats accompanied by rapturous applause from the 14,000-strong crowd, before a cameo by Nessie, pipers and Outlander's Sam Heughan.

Then those supporters briefly turned into the stars of the show.

The same crowd that gleefully welcomed the current British monarch to Glasgow an hour earlier joined pipers and drummers in a stirring, reverberative rendition of Flower of Scotland, the national anthem about King of the Scots Robert the Bruce driving King Edward II's army back at the Battle of Bannockburn.

The pipes were indeed calling. (Getty Images: Mattia Ozbot)

Fiji's noqu masu, New Zealand's haka, Samoa's siva tau (performed by the team as they emerged for the parade) can crack stone hearts and soak eyes that don't cry at funerals.

This was one of those moments.

The sounds of the pipes bouncing off the walls, the power of the drums rattling through the seats and the choral sensation cascading from the upper decks all ended up in the same place: deep in the bones of all those in attendance.

The size, shape and set-up of the venue made it feel like a more intimate performance than the sort of extravaganza intended for hundreds of millions of people to watch on TV.

There was a theme of gardens and nature throughout the ceremony. (Getty Images: Ross MacDonald/SNS Group)

It was enough to make everyone in the arena want to be Scottish, but most of all Commonwealth Sport president Donald Rukare.

"It's good to be back," he said.

The thanks to the 2014 hosts and ensuing elation from the Scottish crowd came with an undertone of side-eye to the slack left by Victoria's 2023 rug-pull.

Both Scotland and Australia pride themselves on punching above their weight, taking things in stride with good humour and getting on with the job.

There was a party atmosphere for the athletes' parade. (Getty Images: Ross MacDonald/SNS Group)

But only one of those countries is putting on these Games. And there does seem to be a genuine feeling of pride in Glasgow for stepping into a tough situation, albeit with help from a few hundred million dollars from Victorian taxpayers.

During his speech, Rukare said he hoped the crowd would indulge him sporting a Wallace tartan kilt, re-imagined in the colours of his native Uganda.

Indulge him? Saltires flew with the sort of fervour usually reserved for the athletes who will take centre stage for the next 10 days.

By the time King Charles officially declared the Games open, the whole event turned into a concert courtesy of Callum Beattie, Tom Walker and KT Tunstall.

The lead-up to these Games has at times shown the hallmarks of a major global event pulled together under an intense time crunch.

But the opening ceremony, aside perhaps from a few eisteddfod-level costumes, overcame that with a passion and spirit that was purely Scotland.

Rukare has spoken about making the Games fit for purpose, even if that means getting smaller in some ways, to remain relevant.

After this opening it's hard to imagine going back.

Do you know an athlete competing at the Commonwealth Games? Share it with us.

title: First suspected positive case of H5 bird flu detected in Queensland
text:
Queensland authorities say they believe a suspected positive case of H5 bird flu has been detected in the state.

Samples from a migratory bird found washed up on Moreton Island have been sent to a CSIRO lab for confirmation.

Queensland Minister for Primary Industries Tony Perrett said a deceased petrel was found by Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service staff in the last 24 hours.

"We are prepared for this, there has been constant communication federally with respect to this," he said.

Mr Perrett said there had been 17 positive cases detected across Australia so far, the majority of which have been in Western Australia.

Queensland chief veterinary officer Alison Crook said it is a "similar scenario" to other cases around Australia.

Queensland chief veterinary officer Allison Cook said authorities have moved into "full stand-up" mode. (ABC News)

"It is very important for me to state that there has been no detection of H5 bird flu in poultry in Australia."

Ms Crook said the Department of Primary Industries had moved into a "full stand-up" after the detection.

She added authorities had received more than 1,000 reports from the public about suspected bird flu cases.

If you see a sick or dead bird: Do not touch it.

Take note of the location and, if possible, take photos.

Report it to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline: 1800 675 888.

"A significant part of our surveillance is community reporting … and I thank the community for their vigilance."

Ms Crook said a suspect positive case means the bird has tested positive for H5 bird flu at the Queensland government biosecurity laboratory, but national protocol requires the sample to be sent to the Centre for Disease Preparedness in Geelong, Victoria.

What we know about bird flu Birds infected with a deadly type of bird flu have been found in Western Australia, South Australia and New South Wales. Where else could birds carrying the virus land?

Confirmation is expected "in the coming days", Ms Crook said.

Mr Perrett said in a statement that the risk to human health remains unchanged.

"It is safe to continue to eat chicken meat or eggs, and I would encourage Queenslanders to purchase these products as they normally would."

Ms Crook said poultry farmers and the poultry industry had been notified and are "well prepared".

"They know what they need to do for their biosecurity and they are doing it."

Invasive Speces Council policy director Dr Carol Booth said the discovery should concern "every Australian who cares about our wildlife".

"Australia has invested heavily in surveillance and early detection, and that preparation is working. But finding cases is only the first step," she said.

"We can't stop this virus, but we can boost the capacity of wildlife to survive and recover from outbreaks."

title: How an insect repellent left almost a dozen hospital staff sick
text:
About a dozen staff became sick at the Flinders Medical Centre emergency department early yesterday morning after the accidental release of an outdoor insect repellent.

Three staff members were hospitalised, with all three later discharged from the hospital yesterday evening.

SA Health chief executive Robyn Lawrence said no patients got sick as a result of the incident.

So, how did we get here?

The incident occurred about 2am yesterday. (ABC News: Daniel Taylor)

How was the repellent released?

The incident occurred about 2am yesterday when a staff member was checking a patient's bag to ensure there was nothing in it that could pose a safety risk to the patient when left alone.

While going through the patient's belongings, the repellent's lid was accidentally knocked off and the aerosol was released.

Dr Lawrence said the product was understood to be a fogger mosquito repellent, which releases an aerosol fog fo insecticide into the air.

Following the incident, the area was cleaned and thought to be under control.

"As staff became unwell, we got concerned we needed additional support to manage the scene to make sure it was safe for staff and our patients," Dr Lawrence said.

Within hours, 11 staff members from the emergency department became unwell after reporting predominantly respiratory symptoms, including shortness of breath and difficulty breathing in.

Three staff members were admitted to hospital, with one in a serious but stable condition.

All three have since been discharged.

Robyn Lawrence says the lid was knocked off the insect repellent by a member of the hospital staff. (ABC News: Evelyn Manfield)

The Metropolitan Fire Service (MFS) arrived at the site about 10am to ensure the area was appropriately cleaned and to determine why ongoing symptoms were occurring in staff.

This included a range of atmospheric tests, and testing of the surfaces and the equipment in those zones to ensure nothing was left contaminated.

Can patients visit Flinders Medical Centre?

Twelve of the 59 beds in the emergency department were closed for safety reasons, but have since reopened.

Dr Lawrence said the hospital was redirecting resources across the system to share the load and take pressure off Flinders Medical Centre during the closure.

What happens now?

The Australian Medical Association in South Australia (AMA) last night said it was aware of the incident.

Peter Subramaniam says he is confident SA Health will investigate the incident properly. (ABC News: Lincoln Rothall)

"We won't comment until the details are released by SA Health," AMA president Peter Subramaniam said.

"[We're] absolutely confident that SA Health will undertake the appropriate processes to ensure that there is an investigation, and any learning from it we would hope will improve our situation.

"At the moment, the focus really is on recovering from this incident and taking care of colleagues who need care."

title: Former Christian Brothers schools to compensate victims of clerical abuse
text:
Advertisement

Some of Australia’s most elite schools will help to pay hundreds of victims of clerical sexual abuse after reaching a deal with the Christian Brothers, which had said it would be broke in months. Documents obtained by this masthead showed the Christian Brothers had previously transferred some of its most valuable colleges and properties to a separate trust, Edmund Rice Education Australia, for just $1 each. The Christian Brothers has for 150 years run schools and orphanages across Australia. AP In Victoria, the transfers of prestigious St Kevin’s College in Toorak, Geelong’s St Joseph’s College and Parade College in Bundoora were all made in June 2015 – just three months before the Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse first recommended the Ellis Defence be dismantled. St Kevin’s has since broken away from Edmund Rice Education Australia. Edmund Rice Education Australia announced on Friday it had agreed to a revised scheme that will compensate victims of historical sexual abuse claims in full.

Advertisement

“Today we are seeking to provide reassurance that we will step in to address future funding shortfalls,” EREA chair Stephen Brown said. Related Article Child abuse Christian Brothers claim no money left to pay sex-abuse survivors “We support a revised scheme because it is simply the right thing to do for victims and survivors to provide them a just and sustainable pathway,” he said. The chair said the “complex arrangements” would take six to eight weeks to document before it was put to creditors to vote on, and the court needed to approve it. Lawyers for the religious order told the NSW Supreme Court earlier this month it estimated it owed $774 million to survivors with current claims and ones that may be filed in future, but only had $216 million worth of assets.

Advertisement

Brown said the 61 schools run by Edmund Rice would continue to operate. Lawyer Laird Macdonald from Rightside Legal said the Christian Brothers’ handling of the matter had exacerbated his clients’ trauma. “Our clients have been through a very tough few weeks. “The idea that the Christian Brothers were broke was always a farce. The attempt to shirk responsibility was disgraceful. We fought hard to keep them accountable, and today they have given up on this appalling plan.” The Christian Brothers said in a statement it meant the educational organisation would settle with claimants but also it would “assume responsibility and liability for all current and future legal proceedings”.

Advertisement

It would also include those before the National Redress Scheme, the religious order said. “The Trustees of the Christian Brothers thank EREA for engaging in this process and agreeing to a pathway which would provide a substantially better outcome for our creditors, including the victims and survivors of abuse.” Earlier this month all sex abuse claims against the religious order in Australia were suspended after NSW Supreme Court Justice Scott Nixon found they would face financial ruin despite the property transfers. The Catholic order was granted a moratorium which also gave survivors time to consider whether they would support a property sell-off by the Christian Brothers to raise funds or pursue other legal avenues. A directions hearing will be held at a later date.
