text:
These are the hottest new breakfast, brunch, bakery and coffee spots, as featured in Good Food’s 100 Essential Melbourne Cafes and Bakeries 2026 guide. See all stories .

It’s easy to get overwhelmed with the selection, but everything is worth trying at Dulce Bakehouse.

8 / 9 It’s easy to get overwhelmed with the selection, but everything is worth trying at Dulce Bakehouse.

The perfect vanilla slice is found at Dulce Bakehouse, where the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche.

1 / 9 The perfect vanilla slice is found at Dulce Bakehouse, where the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche.

Some quests are endless. Others – such as seeking the perfect vanilla slice – can be concluded in Melbourne’s north-western suburbs, specifically at Dulce Bakehouse, a South American bakery that also nails Aussie high-street classics and French pastries. Since 2017, the family-run Delahey shop has been the only place with a queue on a low-key shopping strip.

In a genius South American twist that nods to the owners’ heritage, the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche, the rich milk caramel cutting the slice’s creamy sweetness with a touch of bitterness and tang.

Empanadas are non-negotiable. The beef version is filled with braised, paprika-spiced topside wrapped in lard pastry, along with the quirky and satisfying Chilean-style inclusions of one black olive, two sultanas and a slice of boiled egg. Whatever you land on, you’re unlikely to strike any duds.

Must order: The bestselling vanilla slice, with custard shimmering and quivering between crisp, light puff pastry.

title: Coracle
text:
These are our favourite spots for exceptional breakfast and brunch food, as featured in Good Food’s 100 Essential Melbourne Cafes and Bakeries 2026 guide. See all stories .

Vietnamese hot coffee blends condensed milk, evaporated milk and coconut milk with coffee that’s been dripped through a filter for eight hours.

8 / 8 Vietnamese hot coffee blends condensed milk, evaporated milk and coconut milk with coffee that’s been dripped through a filter for eight hours.

Coracle is perhaps best defined as a fine-dining cafe. There’s coffee and tea, eggs and toast, soup and sandwiches, but everything is rendered with deep thought, pristine ingredients and exquisite care. Just ask the toast: cloud-fluffy Japanese milk bread grilled over binchotan charcoal.

Owners Duong “Jane” Nu Duc Tam and her partner, Wong “Benny” Tat Ying are from Vietnam and Hong Kong respectively, and they’ve embraced a passion for Japanese culture and cuisine. Those three cultures are all represented on the menu, but the bedrock dish is the Japanese breakfast set, a tray of small dishes that includes fish, rice, soup, something fried, something grilled, a little sashimi and pickles.

The Viet coffee is its own feat of creativity: a blend of condensed, evaporated and coconut milks, steamed together with filter coffee. Breakfast really can be the most important meal of the day; Coracle reminds us to slow down and enjoy it.

Must order: The Japanese breakfast set. It’s a premium product and is $55 to account for that.

title: Perth’s cafes and bakeries have never been better – here’s 25 of the best
text:
Advertisement

Eating outEssential cafes and bakeries Perth’s cafes and bakeries have never been better – here’s 25 of the best In part one of the inaugural Essential Cafes and Bakeries guide, we celebrate the veteran venues and trailblazers that continue to blaze. Max Veenhuyzen July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

From the gym junkies to the surfers to the Crossfitters to the health-conscious accountants to the runners to the lycra cowboys to the FIFO workers that clog up airport passenger drop-offs at dawn, Perth folks are no strangers to an early alarm. Which means that cafes aren’t just part of life in P-Town: they’re a way of life. While there’s a utilitarian quality to the cafe genre, there’s room, like any hospitality venture, for excellence and craft too. Recognising such excellence and craft is the reason behind assembling Good Food’s inaugural Essential Perth Cafes and Bakeries guide: a roll call of 50 essential addresses that define Perth cafe and coffee culture circa 2026. To help everyone digest this hit list, I’ve split the guide into two 25-venue lists, each roughly broken into two main categories. List one drops today (July 24) and consists largely of the veteran venues and trailblazers that continue to blaze. The second list – a snapshot of the vanguard and the ones to watch – hits the streets next Friday (July 31). As well as being published on WAToday and across the Good Food network nationally, each venue will also be highlighted via the Good Food app. While an exercise like this is a fine excuse to drink heaps of batch brew, scoff pies and revisit old favourites, it also doubles as an opportunity to break down local cafe and coffee trends and consider how they help us understand this city, the people in it, and the effect(s) one has on the other. I’ve spent a lot of time studying the organic, single origin tea leaves and this is what they said.

Advertisement

Hinata’s brunch set. Jessica Shaver Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up Variety is the spiced latte of life If someone asked me to sum up Perth’s cafe landscape in a single word, I’d choose “diverse”. Compared to the cafe food doing the rounds at the start of the last decade – flat whites and cappuccinos, Eggs Benny and things with goat’s cheese, plus lots of square-shaped plates – modern-day brunching has become infinitely more cosmopolitan, interesting and delicious. Flavours from Japan, Malaysia, Vietnam and other Asian nations now represent a bigger slice of the short-order AM cook’s repertoire. Same goes for dishes and spices from North Africa and the Levant. (See also: “Middle Eastern cooking”) These global influences aren’t just being incorporated into the wider cafe cooking playbook but being celebrated in specialised new venues too. Consider cheery Japanesekissaten Hinata, or the Indonesian breakfast noodle shopBakmi Kochan 88. As a result, the definition of “cafe” has become bigger and looser to reflect the blurring of the lines between restaurant, bar, kiosk, takeaway counter, bakery, and about a dozen other styles of hospitality venue. (But in the interests of managing expectations, I define a cafe as a casual daytime-mostly restaurant or shop that serves breakfast, lunch or brunch, and predominantly coffee and tea.)

Advertisement

The noodles at Bakmi Kochan 88 are made daily - sometimes twice daily. Max Veenhuyzen The rise of craft bread, bakers and baked good Like the rest of the world, Perth has witnessed and benefited from the rise of specialistsandwich shops and bakeries over the past decade. The most interesting – places such as Tuart Hill’s glorious retro-leaningStrollio’s Luncheonette or the Highgate instituteChu Bakery for example – are a combination of both. Not that cafes necessarily have to bake their own sourdough, pastries and croissants, especially when one can call on makers such asMary Street Bakery,Big Loaf orCommon Bakery – wholesale bakeries that prove skill and scale can coexist – to supply and deliver them their daily bread. Chu Bakery in Highgate. Perth coffee is about more than long macs topped up

Advertisement

In addition to better baked goods for all, the rise of new Perth cafe empires – see alsoCommunity Coffee Co and Modus – has also raised coffee standards around town. (Most of the city’s new-wave coffee roasters are side hustles for existing cafes with the move to roasting their owns beans as much about quality control as it is tedious business talk trigger warning, vertical integration of supply chains and cost control.) While this growing local interest in specialty coffee should be applauded, it’s also worth noting that “specialty coffee” is also on the increase with operators using the s-word willy-nilly in a bid to catch customers’ eyes. (Understandable, perhaps, in such a competitive marketplace.) While there aren’t hard and fast rules on what constitutes specialty, it pays to have your wits about you: keep in mind the parable of the (alleged) “cool” uncle or auntie that constantly has to remind their nieces and nephews of their street-smarts. Modus Coffee in Victoria Park. (Speaking of the current cafe climate, it saddens me to see more places only serving coffee and hot drinks in takeaway cups. I get and appreciate that not paying someone to collect and wash cups helps owners manage wage costs, but it also seems wrong to watch somebody carefully whisk high-end matcha and then pour it into a paper cup. Remember to BYO cup.) Chu Bakery owners Ryan and Seren Chu.

Advertisement

Neighbourhood watch All this change plus the post-COVID shift to neighbourhood living has seen real cafe growth in suburbia. In the past, inner-city suburbs such as Mount Lawley, Nedlands, Subiaco and Northbridge plus the Perth and Fremantle CBDs dominated the good cafe and coffee discussion. Today, most postcodes boast at least one place to score a fried chicken burger, matcha latte or glazed doughnut. Although the majority of these places are pitched at the local community, some such as Willetton’s South African wonderlandKalahari or Wangara’s intriguing Burmese lunch barBlue Box are destination venues worth travelling for. Pastries at Common Bakery. Perth cafes are better and more interesting than ever

Advertisement

When it comes to cafes, West Australians have it good. It’s only when you do something as mildly insane as trying to distil Perth’s vibrant cafe and coffee culture into 50 essential addresses that you really understand just how good we have it, especially in the suburbs. This rising tide plus the nature of lists means that hard decisions have had to be made and that many worthy, deserving neighbourhood cafes didn’t make the cut. If one of those cafes happens to be your local, I say sorry. Please accept these recommendations as part of my apology. I have my fingers crossed that they’ll hip you to some new places you might want to add to your eating and drinking rotation. But more importantly, I hope this list captures the excitement and diversity of Perth’s casual morning food and drink scene. To all those that help this city rise and shine, day in and day out, we salute you. Thanks for making us the best morning versions of ourselves possible. All venues are reviewed anonymously. The best way to experience the guide is the Good Food app, where you can search via a map or collections. It’s available as a standalone subscription. Download it from the Apple App Store or the Google Play Store.

title: Get these four things right and you’ll have the perfect pie, every time
text:
Advertisement

Tips & adviceKnow how Get these four things right and you’ll have the perfect pie, every time Remember these four key pillars and your pie will linger in the memory long after the last flake of pastry falls off your shirt. Katrina Meynink July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Pie is a verb. Pie is a religion. A good pie inspires loyalty, heretics, even savage disputes. Mention soggy bottoms, hot water versus suet crusts, tomato sauce or no tomato sauce and watch otherwise level-headed adults lose their minds. These convictions often result in everyone knowing exactly what it takes to make a good pie. But like karaoke, this overstated confidence rarely reflects reality. Rather than let blind optimism fuel a mediocre pie, we need to stick to a few key principles. The anatomy of a great pie, be it sweet or savoury, is universal. Whether you’re making a slow-cooked beef pie (like this one, pictured below), a creamy chicken pie or something laden with fruit, our success comes down to getting four things right. Do this and even a humble mince pie starts punching well above its weight. Katrina’s ultimate beef and Guinness pie ticks all the pie pillar boxes. Katrina Meynink Pillar one: pastry A pie is only ever as good as its pastry. Pastry should leave flakes on your shirt. Strangely, pastry is often treated as little more than a container for the filling, when in reality it is responsible for half the eating experience. It should be crisp enough to hold its shape, flaky enough to shatter beneath your fork and sturdy enough to support the filling without buckling. And the key to pie architecture success: using two types of pastry.

Advertisement

According to Caitlin Gallagher, owner of Jocelyn’s Provisions in Brisbane, the perfect pie is all about the balance of the best ingredients and pastry textures. “Start with an indulgent velvety sour cream pastry base, then for balance top with a light and flaky butter puff pastry with layers that offer crisp and crunch.” The best recipes from Australia's leading chefs straight to your inbox. Sign up Think about your favourite pie. Chances are it isn’t memorable because everything tastes the same. It’s memorable because there’s a surprise every few bites. Then comes the mistake that undoes all that good work: hot filling, aka the fastest route to a soggy base that will buckle as you lift that pie from plate to mouth. Allow fillings to cool before assembling. To further ensure a firm base, particularly with a double crust pie, always brush the inside with an egg wash and blind bake the base until lightly golden. Doing this before adding any filling helps to create a moisture barrier. Katrina’s haloumi spanakopita: Sure to leave pastry flakes on your shirt. Katrina Meynink

Advertisement

Pillar two: seasoning Pastry is buttery, rich and relatively mild. It’s not usually pre-seasoned, meaning the filling has to do the heavy lifting. One of the biggest mistakes home cooks make is seasoning their filling until it tastes “just right” in the saucepan. Once wrapped in pastry, those flavours become muted. The butter (the fat) in the pastry works to round out flavours and chefs often recommend over-seasoning the filling to compensate. A cross-section of a lovingly made pie. Katrina Meynink According to chef Marty Boetz, of Brisbane’s Shortgrain, adding a thickener like corn flour or a basic slurry to thicken the sauce of your filling also really impacts seasoning.

Advertisement

“A thickener softens flavour, so it’s important to taste repeatedly as you cook. Don’t be afraid of it. Use your salt. Also think about sweetness and acidity. A splash of Worcestershire sauce, fish sauce, vinegar, mustard or lemon juice can brighten a rich filling and help carry flavour so that your pie is expertly seasoned. You will be much happier with the end result.” We also shouldn’t forget the role of herbs. Always add at the end for freshness, rather than cooking away their flavour. This will also help add depth and seasoning to your pie. The goal isn’t to make the filling salty or sour. It’s to make it taste like the very best version of itself. Marty Boetz and Jocelyn Provisions’ yellow curry chicken and pumpkin pie. Pillar three: filling If you’ve ever cut into a pie only to watch the filling spill across the plate, you already know why consistency matters. There needs to be moisture without sloppiness, structure without bulging insides.

Advertisement

The best fillings don’t simply taste good; they give your mouth something to do. It should hold together just enough to stay inside the slice while remaining luscious and spoonable. The trick isn’t simply adding more flour or corn flour, it’s about reducing sauces properly before they reach the pastry. Slow cooking naturally concentrates flavour, while gelatine from meat helps create body. Cream-based fillings benefit from cooling before assembling, giving the sauce time to thicken. Think about your favourite pie. Chances are it isn’t memorable because everything tastes the same. It’s memorable because there’s a surprise every few bites. Your filling is an opportunity to create structure and contrast. Sometimes it’s just one unexpected ingredient stopping the whole thing from becoming beige. Tender braised beef loves the sweetness of caramelised onions. Creamy chicken benefits from bursts of peas. Even fruit pies rely on contrast, pairing soft fruit with crunchy sugar-crusted pastry. Sugar crunch pastry: Katrina’s apple and Earl Grey pie. Katrina Meynink

Advertisement

Pillar four: The finish Great pies are elevated by dozens of tiny decisions and the final flourish often transforms a pie from good to memorable. An egg wash should leave pastry deeply golden rather than beige. A scattering of flaky salt catches on buttery pastry layers, adding crunch and little bursts of savouriness. Spices and seeds can be used to hint at what’s inside, while a dusting of sugar on a fruit pie creates a brittle, caramelised crust that cracks satisfyingly under your fork. Steam vents aren’t just decorative; they’re insurance against a soggy lid, but even they can become part of the pie’s personality when cut into leaves, stars or neat little slashes. Katrina’s mushroom stroganoff pot pie. Katrina Meynink Then there are the accompaniments. A spoonful of sharp pickle beside a beef pie. A slick of bright green salsa verde cutting through slow-cooked lamb. Vanilla ice cream surrendering to a still-warm fruit pie. Good pie makers know the finish doesn’t stop when the pie comes out of the oven, it extends to everything that makes you reach immediately for another bite.

Advertisement

Perhaps that’s why pie lovers are so opinionated. Great pies are built on tiny decisions, each one almost invisible on its own, but together they are the difference between a pie you’d politely eat and one you’d happily drive across town for. Before long you’re brushing pastry flakes off your shirt and deciding if a second pie counts as poor judgement or an excellent life decision. title: Yellow curry chicken and pumpkin pies
text:
Step 1 Preheat the oven to 180C fan-forced (200C conventional).

Step 2 To make the yellow curry paste, dry-roast the onion, garlic, coriander roots, turmeric and ginger in a small heavy-based saucepan or wok over medium heat, stirring frequently until coloured and soft, about 8-10 minutes. You want them to caramelise to give the finished curry flavour depth. Scrape into a large bowl with the chillies. Set aside to cool before transferring to a food processor and blending for 3-4 minutes to a smooth reddish-yellow paste. Add water, a tablespoon at a time, if the paste is too dry.

Step 3 To make the spice mix, dry-roast the spices slowly in a small heavy-based saucepan or wok over medium heat until toasted and fragrant. When cool, grind with the peppercorns and salt in a spice or coffee grinder. Stir the spice mix into the paste and mix thoroughly to combine.

Step 4 To poach the chicken, add coconut milk, stock, makrut leaves, fish sauce, oyster sauce and palm sugar to a large saucepan. Bring to a simmer. Taste and season, using sugar and fish sauce as required to balance. Bring to a boil. Add the chicken, then reduce heat to low and gently simmer until the chicken is just cooked through.

Step 5 In a separate large high-sided pan, cook the paste and coconut oil until fragrant. Transfer chicken and poaching liquid to the paste. Simmer, stirring regularly, until the mixture begins to thicken and has reduced by at least a quarter. Stir through the cornflour slurry until fully incorporated and the sauce thickens and generously coats the back of a spoon. Add the roasted pumpkin and stir gently.

Step 6 Grease six 150ml pie tins. Cut six 15cm rounds from shortcrust pastry and six 12cm rounds from puff pastry. Press the shortcrust rounds into pie tins and trim the excess pastry. Divide the chicken mixture between pie tins, brush the edges with the egg wash, then top with puff pastry rounds, pressing the edges together firmly. Brush the top of the pies with egg wash, cut small slits in tops and scatter with salt flakes. Refrigerate for 15 minutes, then bake until golden and pastry is cooked (about 40 minutes).

Step 7 Meanwhile, make the cucumber quick pickle. To make the pickling liquid, combine the rice vinegar, sugar, water and a pinch of salt in a small saucepan and bring to a boil. Remove from the heat when the sugar dissolves and allow to cool. It should taste both sour and sweet.

Step 8 Place the ginger, finely sliced eschalots, and thinly sliced red chilli in a small bowl. Leaving the skin on the cucumber, slice lengthways into quarters. Remove the seeds from the centre. Slice the cucumber at an angle into 5cm dice and mix with the julienned ginger and sliced eschalots and chillies.

title: Brisbane’s cafes and bakeries aren’t what they used to be. They’re better
text:
Advertisement

Eating outFood Brisbane’s cafes and bakeries aren’t what they used to be. They’re better Brisbane’s brunch scene has changed a lot over the past decade. We celebrate the best operators in the inaugural Essential Cafes and Bakeries of Brisbane guide. Matt Shea July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

It’s easy to think it started during the pandemic. Brisbane was shut down. Restaurants and cafes couldn’t operate. So they adapted – fast. For cafes, that meant turning themselves into a coffee window or a bakery or a specialised grocer – whatever it was, as long as it was grab-and-go. Anything to keep some sort of cashflow ticking over. Idle in New Farm. Markus Ravik But adversity has a habit of creating opportunity. Take Pawpaw in East Brisbane, back then still the buzzy engine of the small fiefdom of venues owned and operated by Giorgina Venzin. Venzin turned it into a pop-up bakery with the help of baker Lindsay Krahenbring, who’d found herself out of work as a pastry chef after the shutdown of Hillstone, St Lucia Golf Links’ wedding and functions venue.

Advertisement

Related Article Brisbane’s best cafes and bakeries (on the northside) in 2026 Krahenbring was (and remains) a weapon, Pawpaw’s bakery pop-up turned into a hit, and within a year, Venzin had created a bakery, Darvella, in Bulimba with Krahenbring at the helm. Darvella is now regarded as up there among Brisbane’s best bakeries. Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up The pandemic was full of these sorts of stories: things becoming other things, and then creating other things again. Salud cafe & restaurant in Auchenflower. But you could argue COVID just accelerated what was already happening in Brisbane’s cafe scene by early 2020. Diners were becoming more sophisticated, so the classic full-service, order-at-the-table cafe with a broad menu and well-made espresso wasn’t quite what they were chasing anymore. Not all the time, anyway.

Advertisement

They wanted pour over coffee and batch brew. They wanted ceremonial-grade matcha. Eggs Benedict was fine, sure, but why not omurice or fancy congee or egg drop sandwiches? Pre-made cabinet sandwiches became showstopping chef-made numbers, bakeries began to drill down on kouign-amanns or eclairs or custard tarts. Queen Amann’s specialty? Kouign-amann, naturally. Markus Ravik As for operators, reducing a venue down to a focused offering means better control of overheads in an age when the cost of everything seems to be going up. The result in 2026 is the Brisbane cafe scene has never been more diverse. Breakfast and brunch look a lot different in today’s collection of 30 reviews that make up Part 1 of the inaugural Essential Brisbane Cafes and Bakeries guide of 2026. We live in an age of venues doing one or two things and doing them well, whether it be coffee, matcha, baked sweets or toasties. And it’s in all corners of town.

Advertisement

Sarni’s chef-made sandwiches are some of the best in town. Markus Ravik Mountain River Patisserie has become a mecca for creative pastries and onigiri in Runcorn, Sarni makes some of Brisbane’s best sandwiches in Ascot, and Passport is celebrated for its speciality coffee in Northgate. Don’t get us wrong, there are still great cafes in the traditional sense of the word, but they’re fewer and further between, and often need to do be exceptional to cut through: think Kin in Teneriffe, or Goodfolk in Bardon, or Florence in Camp Hill. Florence sits on a sleepy suburban corner in bucolic Camp Hill. The other trend we’ve noticed recently? The return of the all-day venue – the kind of place that feels right whether it’s serving you brunch, lunch or dinner. Emme is perhaps the most prominent example right now – Tommy Tze Lian’s slick James Street diner easily switching from heaving brunch services to moody dinners – but over the road The Green pulls a similar trick on weekends and, up on Arthur Street, Bistro Tino will do the same come September. Running a hospitality business in 2026 is hard. After years of pandemics and inflation and interest rate fluctuations and natural disasters and war, many operators, if given a chance, will wonder aloud to you why they do it. And that’s a bummer because what they’re doing has never, ever been better.

For subscribers Matt Shea is Food and Culture Editor at Brisbane Times. He is a former editor and editor-at-large at Broadsheet Brisbane, and has written for Escape, Qantas Magazine, the Guardian, Jetstar Magazine and SilverKris, among many others.

title: ‘It tastes phenomenal’: Southside newcomer serves gnocchi, but not as you might know it
text:
Advertisement

Review Eating outSouth Yarra ‘It tastes phenomenal’: Southside newcomer serves gnocchi, but not as you might know it Mudo weaves cornerstone Korean ingredients – gochujang, buckwheat, soju, sesame – into the types of dishes Melbourne diners would recognise from any inner-city restaurant menu. Emma Breheny July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

1 / 8 Gnocchi with gochujang-infused burnt butter. Bonnie Savage 2 / 8 Potato rosti with salted cod roe, creme fraiche, mackerel and caviar. Bonnie Savage 3 / 8 Mudo’s wagyu tartare, seasoned with shallots, chives, soy sauce and sesame oil, is mixed with diced nashi pear. Bonnie Savage 4 / 8 Mudo is housed in the former Atlas Dining site. Bonnie Savage 5 / 8 Duck breast with pumpkin, beetroot, soy plum sauce and kohlrabi kimchi. Bonnie Savage 6 / 8 Nori makguksu - buckwheat noodles with nori vinaigrette and kale. Bonnie Savage 7 / 8 Me-mil - buckwheat cake, doenjang caramel, toasted nuts and seeds, gochujang tuille and sesame ice-cream. Bonnie Savage 8 / 8 The dessert incorporates all three of Korea’s “mother sauces”. Bonnie Savage Previous Slide Next Slide 14 / 20 How we score Mudo Dining & Bar Korean$$$$ Potato gnocchi in a red sauce is placed on the table. Golden from the pan, soft enough to cut with a spoon, the long rectangles of pasta are sprinkled with parmesan. But this isn’t an Italian restaurant. The sauce is stained by gochujang, the earthy Korean chilli paste known for its firepower, except here it’s tamed by browned butter. Mudo chef Happy Cho created this dish with one eye trained on Korea’s chewy oblong-shaped rice cakes – tteokbokki – and the other on his typical customer, more likely to know their gnocchi from their rigatoni. Like a Harry Potter novel, the dish says different things to different groups. The main thing, though, is that it tastes phenomenal. Mudo is housed in the former Atlas Dining site. Bonnie Savage

Advertisement

Cho opened Mudo quietly before Christmas with front-of-house manager Whale Kim and a motto: Aussie vibes, Korean edge. The long dining room is tastefully dressed in creams and caramels. The food, though, is anything but beige. Cornerstone Korean ingredients – gochujang, buckwheat, soju, sesame – are woven into the types of dishes diners would recognise from any inner-city restaurant menu of the past three years. Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up The Korean-born Cho has cooked everywhere from sushi restaurants to the finest of fine diners over two decades. Interning at San Francisco’s Benu in 2019, he saw Korean-American chef Corey Lee weave his culture into menus that scooped up awards. Could Cho also draw on his different experiences as a chef and create something unique? Wagyu tartare features diced nashi pear. Bonnie Savage His steak tartare walks the line between wine-bar favourite and yukhoe, Korea’s dish of raw beef. Wagyu seasoned with shallots, chives, soy sauce and sesame oil is mixed with diced nashi pear, a favourite tenderising ingredient for Korean chefs.

Advertisement

Rich, bronze-skinned duck shares the plate with pumpkin and beetroot, but also lozenges of kohlrabi turned into electrifying kimchi. Take one and consult your doctor if symptoms persist. Buckwheat, grown in Korea far longer than wheat, is as important to the cuisine as butter is to France or masalas are to India. It’s used here in noodles that play counterweight to Mudo’s bigger flavours, and in a sponge cake, torn over salted caramel with sesame ice-cream and a tuile wafer that’s sweet, salty and spicy. Some tables are trying their first ssam, the lettuce wraps that add crunch and contrast to Korean barbecued meat. Others need no introduction to jangajji, the pickles that brighten a barramundi fish burger, or baekseju, the herbal rice wine that replaces Campari in the house negroni. You can ask about unfamiliar ingredients and staff will help, but there were so few waitstaff across my visits that you’re unlikely to get a spiel when you first open your menu. Some people will love that, but in this case, there’s lots to say. Me-mil - buckwheat cake, doenjang caramel, toasted nuts and seeds, gochujang tuille and sesame ice-cream. Bonnie Savage

Advertisement

That sponge cake dish? It incorporates all three of Korea’s “mother sauces”, I later find out. Doenjang, a soybean paste similar to miso, deepens the caramel. Gochujang brings the heat in the wafer. And ganjang, Korean soy sauce, adds brittle crunch to toasted nuts and seeds. Other dishes are there simply because they’re delicious. A neat golden brick of prawn toast is never a bad idea. Potato rosti topped with torched mackerel is two godly bites of fish-and-potato, a timeless combo. Dishes are priced at what can only be called a steal in 2026. A main course of duck for less than $55. A proper, entree-sized plate of gnocchi for $18. Half a chicken with carrot and pickled fennel: $29. People are rightly sensitive about prices, but I wonder if charging a few extra dollars across the menu would pay for another waiter on the floor, and result in a better experience all round. On the way home, I see a billboard advertising Subway’s latest special. It’s a steak sandwich with Korean barbecue sauce. Aussie vibes, Korean edge? The low-down Atmosphere: Softly lit room adds a backdrop to thrills on the plate Go-to dishes: Wagyu tartare ($24); gnocchi with gochujang ($18); duck breast ($42); nori makguksu noodles ($12); me-mil buckwheat cake ($14) Drinks: Cocktails starring Korean rice wine (soju); considered non-alcoholics; affordable Australian, Italian and French wines Cost: About $140 for two, excluding drinks

Advertisement

This review was originally published in Good Weekend magazine. Good Food reviews are booked anonymously and paid independently. A restaurant can’t pay for a review or inclusion in the Good Food Guide.

title: Nathan Cleary drops huge update on playing future
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLNRL 2026 Nathan Cleary drops huge update on playing future Christian Nicolussi Updated July 24, 2026 — 12:00pm ,first published July 24, 2026 — 5:00am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Panthers champion Nathan Cleary has provided a huge update on his playing future, all but ruling out a potential move to the New Zealand Warriors because of fears he could block the path of his little brother, Jett. What Cleary does beyond the 2027 season – and even if he is prepared to head to the open market on November 1 – has become one of rugby league’s biggest storylines. Class act: Nathan Cleary checks in on Tallyn Da Silva. NRL The Warriors, where Cleary’s father Ivan coached and played, has been one rival club regularly linked to the halfback, along with the cashed-up PNG Chiefs and their tax exemptions. Cleary said he had used the past few weeks to settle back into club life at Penrith after leading NSW to a memorable Origin series win, and was no certainty to become a free agent.

Advertisement

The Panthers remain hopeful of getting a deal done, and even sitting down with him and his management in the coming weeks. As for a move to the Warriors, which was floated this week in News Corp, Cleary said: “I grew up [in Auckland] a fair [few years], and I will admit I was a Warriors fan growing up. “But at the end of the day, even if that was an option [to play for them], I’d be blocking my little brother’s pathway. I’m not too sure about [doing] that. “I want to put a bit more thought into it. The last few weeks there have been a bit of a comedown from Origin, and I’ve been trying to get back into a routine with the boys, and get back into consistent training. “That sort of [contract] stuff will work itself out in the background.”

Advertisement

Jett, 21, was in the Penrith system before heading to the Warriors, where he is yet to make his NRL debut. Nathan was glad the Panthers could get the 24-18 win over western Sydney rivals Parramatta on Thursday night, but felt bad one of his kicks slammed into Tallyn Da Silva’s head from close range, forcing him off the field for a head injury assessment, which he later passed. Nathan Cleary’s kick didn’t miss Tallyn Da Silva. Nine There was a nice touch when Cleary made a point of checking in on da Silva as he rolled around in pain while being helped by a trainer in the middle of CommBank Stadium. “I would not like that happening to myself; I wanted to see if he was all right [because] it cranked his neck a bit too,” Cleary said.

Advertisement

“I remember we had a captain’s run, I was practising 40-20s, [former Panther] Api Koroisau was just in front of me, and I did that exact same thing. He was wearing an earring, and the earring popped out. “It was the day before a game, and he was lucky he didn’t get concussed. It was so similar to [the] one tonight.” Da Silva said he would be fine to play next Sunday against his former club the Wests Tigers in what will double as his 50th NRL game. “It felt like a rocket,” Da Silva said of the Cleary cannon. “It hit me in the back of my head, my ears started ringing, and my head was stinging. It really hurt in the moment. It was partially my fault because I turned around and clocked off for a bit.

Advertisement

“Once I stood up and walked around it went away. It’s bound to happen every now and then. I was just unlucky.” The Panthers push on to Mudgee next weekend where they play Canberra. Fullback Dylan Edwards missed the Eels clash because of a shoulder injury – not because of the 10 stitches required for a nasty head gash last week – and is no certainty to return against the Raiders. His replacement, Jaxen Edgar, was excellent in his second NRL game. Winger Tom Jenkins took his season tally to 26 tries, which broke Rhys Wesser’s long-standing club record of 25 set in 2003 – the year Penrith won the premiership. title: Not just phishing: The scams to watch for if you're an Origin Energy customer
text:
In brief As many as 4.8 million Origin Energy customers could be affected by a cyber attack that exposed personal data.

Experts have warned the fallout may go beyond phishing scams.

Nearly five million Australians have been urged to stay vigilant for scams after a cyber attack on Origin Energy saw customer data accessed and disclosed.

Origin — Australia's largest energy retailer, with roughly 4.8 million customer accounts across electricity, gas, LPG and internet services — confirmed that names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and account information were all potentially viewed in the breach.

It said on Thursday it was working to understand how many of its customers were affected.

Also potentially exposed were the last four digits of credit cards and the last three digits of bank accounts.

That combination of data has raised the risk of follow-up scams, particularly phishing attempts that use real account details to appear more convincing.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Phishing scams involve fraudsters posing as a trusted organisation, often via email or text, to trick people into handing over personal details, passwords, or banking information.

But experts warn phishing may not be the only thing customers need to worry about.

How Origin customers can protect themselves

New guidance from the company has urged customers to stay alert for scam emails and texts, designed to deceive people into providing their personal details.

"These fake emails often include a corporate logo and look like they've come from a legitimate company," Origin said.

Anyone who believes they've received a scam email is encouraged to report it to Scamwatch .

READ MORE Origin Energy reports customer bank, credit card details caught up in data breach

Origin has recommended customers keep a strong, unique password for their account and never share it with anyone, stressing it will never ask for a password over the phone or via email.

If a scam email arrives, the company's advice is to not click links, open attachments or forward it on, but to report it directly.

To tell a genuine email or text apart from a fake, Origin says to check for a suspicious sender address, spelling mistakes or bad grammar, and where any links actually lead.

"We've seen scam websites posing under originnergy.com.au so look carefully at the website address," Origin said, adding that the only safe way to pay a bill was via their website or the Origin app.

The company has also flagged fake job offers, and bogus calls or emails requesting orders or parts from people posing as Origin procurement staff, as scams to watch for.

Experts say Origin's response has fallen short

Some experts say the breach is another example of a major company failing to handle a data incident well, and are calling for tough accountability standards for essential service providers.

The breach represents the country's most high-profile cyber incident since Partnered Health, owned by private equity firm Quadrant, said earlier in July that its medical records were breached, affecting 21 of its clinics in Sydney, Melbourne and Canberra.

In 2025, airline Qantas said it had customer data published by cybercriminals, while telco giant Optus and health insurer Medibank were hit in attacks in 2022 that sparked cyber-resilience laws.

READ MORE Australian patient details exposed in GP network cyberattack

"Companies must do better," Jacqueline Boaks from the Centre of Applied Ethics at Curtin University said.

"Delays in notifications, including reports that Origin ignored hackers' emails, passive language ('information may have been accessed') and minimising language (suggesting customers should be less worried about non-payment details being leaked) are extremely disappointing."

Rumpa Dasgupta, a lecturer in cybersecurity at La Trobe University, called it a "disappointing day" for Australian consumers and said concern extends beyond the leak of payment data.

"An electricity bill is more than just an invoice. It can provide valuable insights into a household," she said. "Energy consumption patterns may reveal when residents are typically home or away, the types of appliances they use, occupancy trends, and even periods of extended absence, such as weekends or holidays."

"In the wrong hands, this information could be exploited not only for highly targeted phishing campaigns but also to support physical crimes such as burglary by identifying vulnerable properties."

Rahat Masood, a senior lecturer in cybersecurity at UNSW, said it was "encouraging" to see Origin responding quickly, but that customers expect "robust measures" from a company operating critical infrastructure.

"Even if payment details were not compromised, personal information such as names, addresses, phone numbers and email addresses can still be valuable to cybercriminals and may be used for targeted phishing, identity theft and social engineering attacks," Masood said.

"Incidents like this reinforce that cybersecurity is no longer just an IT issue; it is a core part of operating essential services."

— With additional reporting by the Australian Associated Press.

title: Richard Marles says new US tariff on Australia 'makes no sense'
text:
in brief US President Donald Trump has unveiled sweeping new international trade tariffs.

Australia is one of dozens of countries hit with a new 12.5 per cent levy on trade goods.

Labor leaders have hit back at new tariffs imposed by the United States on Australian goods, which are due to take effect at 2.01pm AEST on Friday.

The new levy is set to replace a temporary 10 per cent tariff that was due to expire at the same time, which the US claims is in an effort to tackle "forced labour".

Defence Minister Richard Marles said that the justification behind the tariffs doesn't make sense.

"This, in our view, makes no sense," Marles told ABC radio on Friday morning.

"Forced labour is definitely something which needs to be combated around the world, so we support that objective.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"But we have laws, which are amongst the strongest in the world, and we’ve been making that point to the Trump administration."

Almost all goods sent from Australia to the US will be impacted — except for those already loaded onto ships en route — at the specific direction of US President Donald Trump, the US trade action states.

Australia is among 54 other economies the US trade department claims have "failed to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor". These include Brazil, China, Egypt, India, Israel, Japan, Norway, Russia, and Singapore.

Other regions, which the US claims have only failed to "enforce" forced labour prohibitions, face a 10 per cent tariff. These include Canada, the European Union, and Mexico.

Trump has made tariffs central to his second term in office — arguing their use will prevent other nations from taking advantage of the US — however, his previous attempt to impose them was struck down by the United States Supreme Court.

Many experts have said the current claims of "forced labour" — and the need to enforce the new measure to level the playing field as the US does not use forced labour — are merely a pretext to re-impose a key policy.

Australia's Trade Minister Don Farrell has called for the tariffs to be removed.

"These tariffs are unjustified, inconsistent with our free trade agreement, and should be removed," he said in a statement to SBS News.

"Australia's measures to combat forced labour and modern slavery are among the strongest in the world, and we are recognised globally, including in the US, for our leadership."

The Australian government previously made a formal request for the US not to proceed with the tariffs earlier this month after they were proposed in June.

title: Not a 'one off spike': Why higher petrol prices are set to return
text:
Key Points Oil prices are expected to rise as the government's reduction to the fuel excise is due to end next month.

Meanwhile, bonds traders ratcheted up expectations that the Reserve Bank will raise interest rates for a fourth time in 2026.

Petrol prices are expected to rise once again, which could cause a crunch at the bowser for Australians already feeling the pinch.

While Australia's resilient labour market continues to be a bright spot in the nation's economy, including the addition of more than 76,000 jobs in June, dark clouds are gathering on the horizon.

After the Australian Bureau of Statistics released another strong labour force report on Thursday, bonds traders ratcheted up expectations that the Reserve Bank will raise interest rates for a fourth time in 2026.

The market is now full priced in for a rate hike by Christmas. A few weeks ago it was a 50-50 bet.

The resurgence in rate fears is not all down to domestic factors.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Renewed hostilities in the Middle East have sent oil prices rising again and threaten to add fresh inflationary pressures to the economy.

While June quarter inflation data next week will be crucial to the RBA's next rates decision in August, the figures could already be dated by the time they are released.

Brent crude oil prices have risen from $US72 ($103) a barrel to more than $US96 ($138) a barrel in recent weeks.

As a rule of thumb, each dollar higher the benchmark oil price results in about a cent per litre increase in petrol prices at the bowser.

Along with an extra 16c a litre added to fuel prices from 2 August because of the full resumption of the federal excise, commuters could soon be looking at petrol prices above $2 a litre once more.

Are higher prices here to stay?

Australians might have to become accustomed to higher fuel prices sticking around, said economists at National Australia Bank.

"Recent developments in the Middle East show that the cost shock looks set to be less of a one-off spike and instead something that proves more grinding and protracted," they said in a research note.

Although jobs growth has remained robust, at 4.4 per cent the unemployment rate is higher than the RBA's June quarter forecast of 4.2 per cent, implying that the labour market is weaker than had been expected.

While higher oil prices risk pushing inflation higher, they also pose risks for growth, said Commonwealth Bank head of Australian economics Belinda Allen.

Victoria's economy is leading the way down, where the state's unemployment rate climbed to 5.1 per cent.

"That's a warning sign that both the Allan and Albanese governments are failing to create the economic conditions businesses need to invest, grow and create jobs," said federal opposition employment spokesperson Jane Hume.

title: Australia’s winter wonderland is becoming increasingly reliant on a backup plan
text:
In brief Australia recorded one its worst starts to a snow season in decades this year.

But some resorts were able to turn lifts on the opening weekend thanks to artificial snowmaking.

As the sun rises over Mount Buller in Victoria's High Country, the hum of snow guns echoes across the mountain, blanketing ski runs in artificial snow.

It's a scene that's becoming increasingly common at Australia's alpine resorts.

As winters become less predictable, operators are investing heavily in snowmaking technology, effectively an insurance policy keeping lifts turning and visitors coming when natural snowfall falls short.

With little natural snow falling until 3 July, one of the slowest starts to winter in decades, several resorts still managed to turn lifts on the traditional June long weekend opening thanks to artificial snowmaking — even if only thin stripes of beginner's terrain were open.

Among them was Mount Buller, which has invested $17 million in snowmaking in the past 25 years.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The resort now operates more than 300 snow guns across almost 80km of ski runs, which can generally operate only at a wet-bulb temperature of -2C or below.

Mount Buller also has five snow factories — shipping container-like buildings capable of producing snow around the clock, regardless of temperature.

Mount Buller has more than 300 snow guns, which produce artificial snow. Source: LightRocket / Ye Myo Khant

"Snowmaking has progressively become more and more significant around the world because it smooths out the variability from season to season," Mount Buller spokesperson Rhylla Morgan told SBS News.

"We haven't had many of those big snow events yet. There have only been a handful so far this season, so snowmaking helps us fill in those gaps.

"It allows us to maintain snow cover across the ski area and steadily build that base, which has enabled us to progressively open more terrain and keep our guests skiing and snowboarding."

Morgan said snowmaking technology — which is both water-and energy-intensive — had improved significantly in the decades since its introduction, allowing resorts to produce higher-quality snow more efficiently.

"The technology has really become very finessed; there are amazing companies around the world working to improve that every year," she said.

Resorts also rely on "snow farming" to extend their seasons by creating snow stockpiles in south-facing and shaded areas, which are then groomed to top up runs.

She said Australia's ski industry — which supports more than 22,000 jobs and contributes billions of dollars to the economy — was continuously adapting to the changing climate.

Why has this season been so slow?

While snowmaking has helped resorts cope with poor natural conditions, it hasn't changed the weather.

After an early July dump of snow, the Spencers Creek measuring site — considered the most reliable site for natural snow depth in NSW — recorded a depth of 68cm on 21 July. A good year would exceed a metre.

Experts say a combination of El Niño, the Southern Annular Mode and long-term climate change has contributed to this year's slow start to the snow season.

Dr Andrew Watkins, a climate scientist and Protect Our Winters science alliance member, said an intensified El Niño had brought more persistent high-pressure systems across south-eastern Australia.

"It's bringing a lot more of those big high-pressure systems that give the clear skies, which means less snow," he said.

The same weather pattern has also produced temperature inversions, where it can be warmer on mountain peaks than lower down the slopes.

Those conditions likely contributed to record July temperatures across Victoria's alpine resorts, with Mount Hotham reaching 12C and Falls Creek 11.5C last weekend — breaking their previous July records of 8.7C and 9.8C by several degrees.

Watkins, who is also an avid skier, said record-breaking temperatures were increasingly common in a warming climate.

"I don't think much surprises us as climate scientists anymore. I'd like to say I'm shocked by it, but I'm not," he said.

"The last decade, and even just this year, looking at what's happened in Europe, what's happened in the UK, and even what's happening in Canada and America right now, breaking records by two or three degrees is happening reasonably often."

Warmer conditions also mean cold fronts that might once have delivered snow are increasingly bringing rain instead, degrading the snow pack.

But the high-pressure systems can produce cold, dry nights — ideal conditions for snow guns.

"That's sort of a perfect scenario for snowmaking … which has been a saviour in many places," he said.

A 2024 report by the Australian Mountain Research Facility and Australian National University found snow cover had reduced by 30 per cent between 1954 and 2021, while the length of the ski season had contracted between 17 and 28 per cent at most resorts.

Despite the grim picture, Watkins said snowmaking would still be viable under a warming in the depths of winter.

"In the middle of the season, I think snowmaking has great potential," he said.

“[But] our seasons are still going to get shorter," he said.

As for this snow season, Bureau of Meteorology senior meteorologist Jonathan How said it would only take one big weather system to turn the fortunes around.

"We're entering the back end of winter, so certainly you know we could see some systems coming through still."

title: The US' $166 billion refund comes with a much bigger cost
text:
In brief Despite a Supreme Court ruling against him, Donald Trump uses tariffs to create a sustained economic uncertainty.

The cost of that uncertainty could lead to unforeseen changes to longstanding economic order.

An Australian clothing company, Nashie, recently received a six-figure refund from the US government, including 6 per cent interest, for tariffs it had paid on US imports. It’s just one of thousands of businesses reclaiming tariffs imposed on what President Donald Trump called "Liberation Day".

Of the roughly US$166 billion (AU$237 billion) due to be refunded, more than US$85 billion (AU$121 billion) has already been returned to businesses.

But while a US Supreme Court ruling in February reversed the tariffs, much of the economic and political damage, and perhaps some of the Trump administration’s gains, will be hard to undo.

Tariffs not designed to last

Trump’s April 2025 tariffs relied on the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), a 1977 law that lets presidents respond to extraordinary foreign threats — yet never mentions the word "tariff".

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

On 20 February 2026, the US Supreme Court ruled in a 6-3 decision that IEEPA did not authorise tariffs — under the Constitution, only Congress has the power to impose taxes and duties.

The decision placed an important limit on the president’s power to impose tariffs. But it did not end Trump’s tariff policy, which was, from the beginning, designed to buy time to rewrite US trade relations with the rest of the world.

Trump responded with a temporary 10 per cent global tariff, which expires on 24 July after the statutory 150-day limit.

Trump has also pursued more targeted tariffs under laws dealing with unfair trade practices, national security and anti-slavery claims.

These require investigations into foreign trade practices and lengthy procedures, which slow the process and also create a record that can be scrutinised in court.

The US Trade Representative’s findings on alleged forced labour have prepared the ground for a 12.5 per cent tariff on exports from 54 countries, including Australia, China, New Zealand and the United Kingdom, starting on 24 July 2026.

So while the Supreme Court closed one legal door, the Trump administration was already preparing to walk through another. And it has bought itself yet more time.

The fiscal cost for the US

The refunds paid out contributed to a US$120 billion (AU$171 billion) federal deficit this June, in sharp contrast to the US$27 billion (AU$38 billion) surplus in June 2025.

That said, the US$166 billion (AU$236 billion) being paid out is not a fine. Much of it is money the government collected without legal authority and must now return. This is real money, but it’s less than 2 per cent of the projected 2026 federal government spending.

Without any fanfare to match "Liberation Day", the political cost may be smaller still.

The refunds involve a technical process and are dispersed among many importers, making them far less visible than the original tariff announcements.

And concessions that trading partners, including the UK and the European Union, made under the threat of higher tariffs don’t automatically vanish when a tariff is struck down.

What’s in a refund?

For businesses, repaying money they should never have paid doesn’t recreate the position they were in before the tariffs.

Importers had capital tied up for months as financing costs mounted, orders were cancelled, and inventory sat stranded. Many had to renegotiate contracts.

They paid customs brokers and lawyers, and delayed other investments while waiting to learn which tariff would apply. Some stopped shipping to the US altogether, unsure what customs duties they would incur.

Smaller firms were especially exposed. They generally have smaller cash reserves, fewer alternative suppliers, and less capacity to absorb an unexpected increase in duty.

One analysis estimated small business importers in the US each paid US$306,000 extra in tariffs on average.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta estimates that "financially constrained" businesses will receive 34 per cent of all refunds, or about US$56 billion. These firms are most likely to use the money to invest, hire staff or reduce prices.

Better-financed businesses are more likely to save it, repay debt or distribute it to shareholders. PepsiCo said it would use the refunds to offset some commodity inflation.

But few are likely to pass the refunds back to consumers due to the complexity and cost involved. Nintendo is being sued by its own customers, who argue the tariff refunds should be paid to consumers to offset the higher prices they paid.

Access is also uneven. A business with a US bank account and customs broker can get its refund relatively smoothly. Australian businesses shipping via Australia Post face further delays: those shipments weren’t entered through the system US Customs and Border Protection uses for refunds, and no refund pathway exists yet.

Nor does the refund necessarily reach whoever ultimately paid. Rather, it goes to the importer, even where the cost was passed on to a retailer or consumer through higher prices.

The deeper cost is uncertainty

The most important consequence of Trump’s tariff policy is persistent uncertainty.

Trump can impose an immediate commercial disruption, then use that disruption as leverage in negotiations with other governments. The latest example is Monday’s 50 per cent tariff on a range of Canadian goods, which the US says responds to Canadian retaliation and discrimination against US cars, dairy and alcohol.

Persistent uncertainty also encourages global commerce to fragment into competing blocs whose market access is more predictable.

READ MORE What Trump's 100 per cent pharmaceutical tariff means for Australia

As our Weaponised Trade project shows, the greater danger is other governments adopting the same playbook: temporary, legally questionable trade restrictions becoming the norm rather than the exception.

Litigation to roll back these measures takes months or even years.

And if a court does eventually invalidate a measure, the US can invoke another law and restart the process. Courts operate retrospectively. Markets, on the other hand, react to them immediately.

The Supreme Court ruling did not return lost customers, reverse commercial decisions or restore confidence in predictable trade rules.

Tariffs can be refunded. Uncertainty cannot.

Markus Wagner is Professor of Law at the University of Wollongong School of Law.

title: Origin Energy reports customer bank, credit card details caught up in data breach
text:
In brief Origin Energy has confirmed a recent cyber attack included unauthorised access to customer data.

Customer credit card and bank account details may have been accessed in the breach.

A cyberattack on Origin Energy has included unauthorised access and disclosure of customer data, the energy giant says, a day after it revealed it was caught up in the breach.

The Sydney-based company said on Thursday it was working to understand how many of its almost five million customers were affected by the privacy incursion.

Names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and account information could all have been viewed in the breach, Origin said in an ASX statement.

Also at risk were the last four digits of credit cards, or the last three digits of bank accounts.

Origin chief executive Frank Calabria apologised to customers and said the company was working to contact those affected.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The company has set up a contact number and "additional resources to help manage our response to this incident".

"One of our key priorities is taking action to secure our systems and ensure no further unauthorised access," Calabria said.

"We are working with independent cyber experts to support Origin, and that work is continuing alongside the work of authorities."

The company continued to engage with federal cyber and information agencies on the incident as well as police, according to the statement.

Origin, which has 4.8 million customer accounts in Australia and provides electricity, natural gas, LPG and internet services, said on Wednesday it did not believe the data obtained included credit card or bank details.

Griffith University's Graeme Hughes, an expert on business and consumer issues, said the breach was unlikely to cause cases of payment fraud but marked a "social engineering problem".

READ MORE Millions of Australian customer details potentially exposed after Origin Energy hacked

"The last four digits for a card, a date of birth, and an authentic billing history are the exact trust signals a businesses uses to verify itself over the phone," Hughes told AAP.

"That makes an unsolicited call about an energy account far more convincing than it should be.

"If comparable Australian breaches have taught us anything, the confirmed scope (of the breach) usually widens rather than narrows."

The breach represents the country's most high-profile cyber incident since Partnered Health, owned by private equity firm Quadrant, said earlier in July that its medical records were breached clinics in Sydney, Melbourne and Canberra.

In 2025, airline Qantas said it had customer data published by cybercriminals, while telco giant Optus and health insurer Medibank were hit in attacks in 2022 that sparked cyber-resilience laws.

title: Full list: Here's who will get $338 weekly payments under prac payment expansion
text:
In brief Hundreds of delegates have converged on Adelaide for the three-day conference, which sets out the party's agenda and policy stances.

Anthony Albanese outlined the expanded payments, made several other announcements, and criticised One Nation.

Paid practical placements are being rolled out to university students in 10 frontline allied health fields under a major Commonwealth expansion.

From July next year, eligible students will receive up to $338.60 a week, with the program forecast to cost $745 million by the end of the decade.

The payments build on an existing scheme for teaching, nursing, midwifery and social work students introduced just over a year ago.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese used the first day of Labor’s 50th National Conference to announce the new measure, which he said will provide cost of living relief for students and support the future healthcare workforce.

Albanese addressed the conference to outline the expanded payments, make several other announcements, and criticise One Nation.

Which students will receive the payments?

They are:

Clinical psychology

Physiotherapy

Occupational therapy

Speech pathology

Paramedicine

Radiography

Pharmacy

Rehabilitation therapies

Audiology

Podiatry

The prime minister said it is "surprising" such a scheme doesn’t already exist.

"Paid prac is already making a real difference, it’s helping students with the cost of living while they train and that’s why we’re expanding it," he said on Thursday.

"This is an investment in our future healthcare workforce. We need more of them, and this will help more Australians get the qualifications our nation needs."

Albanese outlines party goals

Hundreds of delegates have converged on Adelaide for the three-day conference, which sets out the party's agenda and policy stances.

Albanese said it was up to Labor to show the difference the government was making to people's lives, rather than just airing grievances.

READ MORE A dark chapter of Australia's past is resurfacing in immigration politics

"People's genuine frustrations deserve a practical response, not just an emotional one," he said.

"You can go a long way in politics, for a long time, by raging against a system that you are part of, without a plan to fix it ... that doesn't build anything."

Albanese stressed the need to tackle long-term challenges as well as deal with daily issues.

"Our focus remains squarely on helping with the cost of living, on the practical measures that matter to people and make a positive difference to their lives," he said.

"That is how we demonstrate to Australians that their hard work will pay off."

Albanese also used the speech to hit out at One Nation, which has polled above Labor in some recent voter surveys.

"All those attention-seeking culture wars imported from overseas, all those trips around the world to talk Australia down, cannot disguise the fact that none of them have anything new or anything positive to offer our country," he said.

The prime minister also announced the creation of a Fair Work Court aimed at resolving workplace disputes.

While the 50th Labor conference is not expected to be confrontational, several major policy issues will be on the agenda for debate.

Among them will be artificial intelligence protections, gas taxes, gambling reform and the party's stance on Palestine.

Attendees at the conference will agree on party positions, but it will be up to Labor's parliamentary team to determine how, and when, measures are implemented.

title: The superannuation 'blind spot' funding Australian gambling companies
text:
In brief Advocates say Australians may not be aware their super fund is investing billions into the gambling industry.

Some super funds do not apply investment screens to gambling companies.

Australia's 20 biggest superannuation funds have almost $15 billion invested in gambling-related companies, despite growing concern about the social harm caused by the industry.

A report released on Thursday by the Alliance for Gambling Reform found the sector holds $14.8 billion in gambling stocks.

Australia's largest fund, Australian Super, has the biggest exposure at $4.9 billion — more than double any other fund — while Australian Retirement Trust, Colonial First State, UniSuper and Aware Super round out the top five.

Gambling company Aristocrat Leisure is just one of the companies into which retirement funds are flowing.

Despite many funds having responsible investment policies, approaches to gambling-related risks remain "limited, inconsistent and largely inadequate", according to chief executive of the Alliance for Gambling Reform, Martin Thomas.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

He said many Australians would be shocked to learn how much of their money was being invested into the industry.

"There's starting to be a realisation just of how socially damaging gambling is," he told the Australian Associated Press.

"We see bankruptcies, mental health issues, marriage break-ups."

Harms from gambling are not consistently treated the same way as harms arising from other industries such as tobacco and alcohol, he said, with the latter routinely screened out of superannuation investments.

Australians losing billions to gambling

Australians are losing more money per capita to gambling than any other country — about $32 billion a year.

A spokesperson for Australian Super said apart from its Socially Aware option, the fund does not apply investment screens to gambling companies.

"We invest to help members achieve their best financial position in retirement," they said.

"We engage with certain ASX-listed companies either directly or with other investors to better understand their initiatives to deliver responsible gaming practices and appropriate governance practices."

Thomas called gambling a "blind spot" for superannuation companies.

Super funds could make more ethical choices about where they put their money, he said, noting gambling investment was a relatively small part of their overall investments, which may range in the hundreds of billions of dollars.

"There is so much opportunity out there for them to do the right thing," he said.

Thomas urged those who were concerned to contact their super fund to find out how much they were investing in gambling.

"We're not looking to ban gambling; we just think it's a harmful legal adult product and should be regulated," he said.

"But we do think it should be shunned by the investment community, particularly super funds, particularly because we're all made to contribute to these super funds."

Gambling reforms announced

Gambling reforms and government support for affected Australians were announced earlier this year.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has confirmed his government intends to table laws to limit gambling ads across various forms of media, including on online platforms unless users have been verified to be over the age of 18.

The federal budget itself has also identified specific measures.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers said $39 million has been set aside over the next four years to improve the availability and reach of financial counselling services, while $28.7 million will be spent on BetStop, in part to strengthen data-matching systems.

The government plans to spend $22.4 million to develop a national online awareness campaign to encourage people affected by gambling harms to seek support.

Readers seeking support for gambling concerns can contact the National Gambling Helpline on 1800 858 858 for free, professional and confidential support 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. More information is available at www.gamblinghelponline.org.au

title: 2026 Census: When it is, how to complete it and who must take part
text:
Census night is Tuesday 11 August, and every person in Australia — including Norfolk Island, the Cocos (Keeling) Islands and Christmas Island — must be counted.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) conducts a Census of Population and Housing every five years to gather what officials describe as a snapshot of the nation.

It includes up to 66 questions spanning a person's ancestry, religious affiliation, sex recorded at birth and even how they commuted to work that day.

The last Census was held on 10 August 2021.

Emily Walter, a Census national spokesperson at the ABS, said many people will be completing the Census for the first time.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"There's a lot of Australians who won't have done a Census before, and this is really important to make sure that people are aware and understand what a Census is, and why it's important," she told SBS Urdu.

How do you complete the Census?

From late July, households will receive instructions on how to complete the Census. Australians can complete the form as soon as they receive instructions, provided they know where they will be on Census night.

Most people will receive a letter with instructions to complete the Census online. However, Australians can also request a paper form if they prefer.

The Census form is available in English. The ABS provides translated information and interpreting services for different languages. Source: Supplied / Australian Bureau of Statistics

Generally, one person per household will complete the Census, but separate forms can be requested for privacy reasons.

Who must be included in the Census?

The Census must be completed by everyone in the country, including Australian residents, international visitors, students, and people on temporary visas.

READ MORE The questions that will change in Australia's 2026 Census

Walter said the ABS expects a response from every person in the country on Census night.

"Sometimes people forget to put babies on their form. So, it's really important that absolutely everyone is reflected in the Census," she said.

What help is available to complete the Census?

Some people living in remote areas of Australia, or experiencing homelessness, can complete the Census with the assistance of field staff.

The ABS has hired around 30,000 field staff to support participation. In 2021, field staff travelled more than 10 million kilometres across Australia.

Liz Allen, a demographer from the Australian National University's Centre for Social Policy Research, said: "That especially includes people in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities where ABS data collectors actually go out in force, and they ... talk to folks about the Census, what it means and to answer any relevant questions."

Information about the Census is also accessible in 25 languages, with free translation and interpreting services available for people completing the Census.

What if you're travelling on Census night?

If a person is travelling within Australia on 11 August, they are still expected to complete the Census.

This includes anyone travelling overnight on a long-distance train, bus, or aircraft (including red-eye flights between Perth and the east coast), and on long-haul transport vehicles that are parked or in motion.

International visitors to Australia are also expected to complete the Census. The form is available at hotels and motels, as well as online in places with internet access. Source: AAP / Joel Carrett

People must include information about where they are staying or whether they are in transit.

If an Australian is overseas on 11 August, they do not need to complete the Census.

If someone is completing the Census form at their usual address, the person travelling needs to be marked as 'away' but usually living at the dwelling.

Can you be fined for not completing the Census?

The Census is compulsory under the 1905 Census and Statistics Act.

If a person does not complete the Census when directed, they will receive a formal written direction to do so. If they refuse, they can be prosecuted and fined up to $364 a day.

Knowingly providing false or misleading statements is an offence and can result in a fine of up to $3,640, according to the ABS.
