title: Shady's back: New twist in Eminem's legal stoush with Australian swimwear brand
text:
In Brief The rapper had until 22 July to launch an appeal against the finding in favour of the Australian beach brand.

The appeal drags out the battle over the US rapper's intellectual property in Australia.

Lawyers for United States rapper Marshall Mathers, better known as Eminem, have filed an appeal after losing a trademark battle against Australian beach brand Swim Shady.

The Australian Registrar of Trade Marks initially ruled in favour of Swim Shady in early July, giving the rapper's legal team until 22 July to lodge an appeal.

The rapper had launched the dispute over similarities to his alter ego 'Slim Shady' — a persona Mathers developed to give voice to his more rage-fuelled raps, such as his controversial 1999 Slim Shady LP album.

The Australian beach brand was founded in 2024 by Jeremy Scott, a former sponsorship manager at the South Sydney Rabbitohs NRL club, and partner Elizabeth Afrakoff, and makes beach shades and accessories.

Documents were lodged with the Federal Court of Australia's Victoria Registry on Wednesday afternoon.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The rapper's legal team, Davies Collison Cave, has been contacted for comment.

Hi! My name is...

Eminem's legal team claimed Mathers has used the word 'Shady' in his promotion since 1999, with the rapper launching an associated clothing brand called Shady Limited.

In Australia, Eminem has owned the trademarks "Shady" and "Shady Limited" for use in merchandise since 2002.

It wasn't until January 2025, however, that the rapper filed for trademark rights to "Slim Shady" — a month after the launch of the Australian company.

Intellectual property law firm Meyer West IP, acting for Swim Shady, filed a 'no-use' application for a partial removal of the rapper's trademarks, arguing that they hadn't been used in Australia for the previous three years.

The judge was not satisfied Eminem demonstrated sufficient control over his image and his business enterprises, and saw no evidence of "direct use" of his trademarks in Australia during the three-year period.

The US mogul was ordered to pay Swim Shady's legal costs.

"Elizabeth and I are delighted with today's decision," founder Scott told Rolling Stone in a statement at the time.

"While this is an important milestone for Swim Shady, it is one step in the broader trade mark proceedings, and we recognise there are still matters to be resolved."

SBS News has contacted Swim Shady for comment.

'Eminem Esque'

In 2017, Eminem took New Zealand's National Party to the Wellington High Court over their use of his track 'Lose Yourself' in a political campaign.

The High Court ordered the National Party to pay NZD$600,000 ($499,000) in damages to Eminem after using a version of the iconic guitar riff, drum beat and sparse piano melody of Lose Yourself in a re-election campaign for former prime minister John Key.

The production music track was called "Eminem Esque".

The battle made it to US late-night talk show Last Week Tonight, where host John Oliver called it "unquestionably the lamest rap beef since Lil Wayne's five-year feud with William H Macy".

US singer Katy Perry lost a 12-year legal battle against Australian fashion designer Katie Perry this year. Source: AAP / Europa Press

Eminem isn't the only celebrity to launch a trademark battle in Australia.

An 18-year trademark dispute between pop singer Katy Perry and Australian fashion line Katie Perry ended in 2026 after the High Court found the designer was not in breach of trademark laws.

The pop singer initially won her case in the Federal Court, only to lose on appeal.

title: 'Truth-teller': Rebel Wilson victorious in defamation trial brought by The Deb co-star
text:
In brief Rebel Wilson's legal battle with co-star Charlotte MacInnes has ended.

Wilson repeatedly denied MacInnes' claims that she had defamed her former co-star.

After a blistering dispute that spanned continents and years, Hollywood A-lister Rebel Wilson has been found not to have defamed her co-star in a series of social media posts.

The Pitch Perfect star was sued by Australian actor Charlotte MacInnes, who played the lead role in Wilson's directorial debut film The Deb.

MacInnes claimed Wilson defamed her in a series of social media posts that implied she was a liar and a sellout who walked back a sexual harassment complaint to further her career.

The posts alleged the young actor confided in Wilson — and later recanted — that she felt uncomfortable when the film's co-producer Amanda Ghost asked to have a shower and a bath together.

MacInnes denied making or retracting a complaint, insisting she had not been uncomfortable when the pair shared a bath in their swimwear after Ghost suffered a medical episode in September 2023.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Two months after the conclusion of the hotly contested court fight in the Federal Court, Justice Elizabeth Raper found in favour of Wilson on Wednesday.

"MacInnes has failed to establish that the publications have caused or were likely to cause serious harm," she said.

MacInnes' barrister Sue Chrysanthou accused Wilson of a "complete revision of history" littered with dishonesty during her emphatic closing address in May.

"She is a fantastical liar who has made up terrible, terrible allegations about multiple people and her own witnesses have discredited her," she said.

"We say Ms Wilson cannot be believed on anything she has said."

But Wilson repeatedly denied accusations she fabricated the complaint or raised it for leverage during disputes with her co-producers over the film's budget and writing credit.

"I am known as a truth-teller," she declared during the hearing.

That was what she had been doing when she posted the stories to her 11 million followers, the Bridesmaids star said.

"I don't classify it as an attack, I classify it as telling the truth," she told the court.

Her barrister, Dauid Sibtain, said his client had "no motive to lie" about something that would endanger her film by driving a wedge between her lead actor and a producer.

Instead, he accused MacInnes of dishonestly withdrawing the complaint about Ghost to ensure her relationship with the producer continued to be fruitful.

MacInnes had received a lead role in a stage production and a six-figure record deal that were linked to Ms Ghost, he argued, and she wanted to ensure she continued to rise.

"Ms MacInnes is someone who gave false evidence under oath," he said.

"(She) has suffered no harm, let alone serious harm."

The young actor sought aggravated damages for the harm allegedly wrought by the posts, including anxiety and a period without work.

title: The age Australians think is 'too old' to still live with your parents
text:
A new survey has revealed the age Australians think is "too old" to still be living at home — and the findings land as rents climb and rental vacancies stay tight.

The survey, from financial comparison site Finder, asked 1,010 Australians to weigh in on how old was "too old" to be still living in the family home, with the average Australian saying 31-years-old was the cut-off date.

It found gen z and baby boomers held the harshest views, both agreeing that 30 was too old to still be living with family.

Millennials and gen x were slightly more forgiving, nominating 32 as the cut-off.

Parents of under 18s were also stricter (30) than those without children (32).

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

But Finder's personal finance expert Sarah Megginson says those numbers don't reflect the financial reality many Australians are living through.

"We know renters are doing it tough — Finder's data shows 47 per cent of Aussie tenants struggled to pay their rent in June," Megginson told SBS News.

"In many suburbs, renters are spending half of their weekly income or more just on rent, and after other bills are paid there’s not much left over for groceries and simply living."

The accepted benchmark for rental stress is spending 30 per cent of income on rent — a threshold Megginson says many Australians would now consider "welcome relief" by comparison.

A new survey suggests 31-years-old is the cut-off date for living in the family home. Source: SBS News

She says the housing crisis is pushing back the traditional markers of adulthood.

"The traditional milestones of adulthood are being pushed further back, with many people delaying moving out, buying a home and starting a family," she said.

It's not necessarily a new trend, either — though data does suggest it's accelerated in recent years.

A July report from research firm McCrindle found young Australians were living with their parents for longer. In 2006, 60 per cent of 19-year-olds lived at home — but by 2021, that figure climbed to 70 per cent.

Their analysis found that young Australians were being challenged with high levels of student debt and a competitive housing market — with some returning to the family nest after moving out to accelerate savings for a housing deposit.

Almost three in five Australians (57 per cent) said they're open to or would welcome living in a multi-generational home with their family — defined as three or more generations living under the same roof.

Twelve per cent currently live in a multi-generational home, McCrindle found, while 32 per cent have done so in the past — with those who speak a language other than English at home significantly more likely to live in this way.

People living in multi-generational households previously told SBS' The Feed the arrangement can help families share costs and caring responsibilities, while providing greater security as relatives age. But some said Australian homes were not well designed to accommodate several generations.

Different cultural backgrounds also played a role in attitudes towards multi-generational living, they said.

Rents high, vacancies low

The pressure to move out of home is playing out against a rental market that's becoming steadily less forgiving.

Nationally, rents rose 3.08 per cent quarter-on-quarter in June and 6.35 per cent over the year, according to REA Group, taking the median weekly rent for a dwelling to $670.

Perth recorded the steepest annual rise, up 10.29 per cent to a $750 median, followed by Hobart (up 9.09 per cent, to $600) and Darwin (up 7.69 per cent, to $700).

Nationally, the weekly median rent for a dwelling is sitting at $670 — and $690 across combined capital cities. Source: SBS News

Sydney remains the most expensive capital, with rents up 6.67 per cent to an $800 median, ahead of Brisbane ($695, up 6.92 per cent), the ACT ($650, up 4.84 per cent), Adelaide ($625, up 4.17 per cent) and Melbourne ($600, up 5.26 per cent).

Vacancies are just as tight.

The national vacancy rate ticked up slightly in June, to 1.26 per cent, but that's still far below where it sat five years ago — down 37 per cent nationally, and 47 per cent in capital cities specifically.

Nationally, vacancy rates are sitting at 1.26 per cent — a slight increase monthly, but still down 37 per cent over the last five years. Source: SBS News

Darwin (0.70 per cent) and Hobart (0.72 per cent) have the least available rental stock of any capital, while Melbourne (1.51 per cent) and the ACT (1.50 per cent) have the most breathing room.

Sydney's vacancy rate sits at 1.30 per cent, though it recorded the biggest monthly jump of any capital, up 0.14 percentage points.

Why staying home longer is becoming the norm

REA Group economist Luc Redman says that squeeze is precisely what's keeping young Australians at home for longer.

"The rental market remains tight nationally despite some easing since the start of 2026," Redman told SBS News.

"The rate of rental price increases certainly would be deterring consumers from entering the rental market and looking for other options where possible."

"This has meant that younger people stay in their parents' home for longer."

He says the shift has a broader impact.

"That has a broader negative impact on the economy when economically productive young people aren't able to move closer to economic opportunities," he said.

"This has certainly shifted over the past few decades as the income of younger people hasn't been able to keep up with rent price increases."

Separately, July Cotality research found households are now devoting close to a third of their income to rent — a record high — compared to around 27 per cent five years ago.

That sits alongside July data from the OECD, which found Australians' real wages have fallen 5.1 per cent since March 2021, one of the steepest declines among developed economies — making rental hikes sting even more.

Australians born in the 1990s are the first generation on record to be no better off than the one that preceded them, according to the Productivity Commission.

Megginson says living at home longer may now be the smarter financial move.

"Living with your parents at 31 may raise eyebrows, but struggling to afford rent is becoming the norm," she said.

"Living at home longer can be a smart financial move, allowing young adults to save a deposit faster and avoid the pressure of soaring rents."

title: Former Christian Brothers schools to compensate victims of clerical abuse
text:
Advertisement

Some of Australia’s most elite schools will help to pay hundreds of victims of clerical sexual abuse after reaching a deal with the Christian Brothers, which had said it would be broke in months. Documents obtained by this masthead showed the Christian Brothers had previously transferred some of its most valuable colleges and properties to a separate trust, Edmund Rice Education Australia, for just $1 each. The Christian Brothers has for 150 years run schools and orphanages across Australia. AP In Victoria, the transfers of prestigious St Kevin’s College in Toorak, Geelong’s St Joseph’s College and Parade College in Bundoora were all made in June 2015 – just three months before the Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse first recommended the Ellis Defence be dismantled. St Kevin’s has since broken away from Edmund Rice Education Australia. Edmund Rice Education Australia announced on Friday it had agreed to a revised scheme that will compensate victims of historical sexual abuse claims in full.

Advertisement

“Today we are seeking to provide reassurance that we will step in to address future funding shortfalls,” EREA chair Stephen Brown said. Related Article Child abuse Christian Brothers claim no money left to pay sex-abuse survivors “We support a revised scheme because it is simply the right thing to do for victims and survivors to provide them a just and sustainable pathway,” he said. The chair said the “complex arrangements” would take six to eight weeks to document before it was put to creditors to vote on, and the court needed to approve it. Lawyers for the religious order told the NSW Supreme Court earlier this month it estimated it owed $774 million to survivors with current claims and ones that may be filed in future, but only had $216 million worth of assets.

Advertisement

Brown said the 61 schools run by Edmund Rice would continue to operate. Lawyer Laird Macdonald from Rightside Legal said the Christian Brothers’ handling of the matter had exacerbated his clients’ trauma. “Our clients have been through a very tough few weeks. “The idea that the Christian Brothers were broke was always a farce. The attempt to shirk responsibility was disgraceful. We fought hard to keep them accountable, and today they have given up on this appalling plan.” The Christian Brothers said in a statement it meant the educational organisation would settle with claimants but also it would “assume responsibility and liability for all current and future legal proceedings”.

Advertisement

It would also include those before the National Redress Scheme, the religious order said. “The Trustees of the Christian Brothers thank EREA for engaging in this process and agreeing to a pathway which would provide a substantially better outcome for our creditors, including the victims and survivors of abuse.” Earlier this month all sex abuse claims against the religious order in Australia were suspended after NSW Supreme Court Justice Scott Nixon found they would face financial ruin despite the property transfers. The Catholic order was granted a moratorium which also gave survivors time to consider whether they would support a property sell-off by the Christian Brothers to raise funds or pursue other legal avenues. A directions hearing will be held at a later date.

title: Dulce Bakehouse
text:
These are the hottest new breakfast, brunch, bakery and coffee spots, as featured in Good Food’s 100 Essential Melbourne Cafes and Bakeries 2026 guide. See all stories .

It’s easy to get overwhelmed with the selection, but everything is worth trying at Dulce Bakehouse.

8 / 9 It’s easy to get overwhelmed with the selection, but everything is worth trying at Dulce Bakehouse.

The perfect vanilla slice is found at Dulce Bakehouse, where the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche.

1 / 9 The perfect vanilla slice is found at Dulce Bakehouse, where the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche.

Some quests are endless. Others – such as seeking the perfect vanilla slice – can be concluded in Melbourne’s north-western suburbs, specifically at Dulce Bakehouse, a South American bakery that also nails Aussie high-street classics and French pastries. Since 2017, the family-run Delahey shop has been the only place with a queue on a low-key shopping strip.

In a genius South American twist that nods to the owners’ heritage, the bottom layer is spread with dulce de leche, the rich milk caramel cutting the slice’s creamy sweetness with a touch of bitterness and tang.

Empanadas are non-negotiable. The beef version is filled with braised, paprika-spiced topside wrapped in lard pastry, along with the quirky and satisfying Chilean-style inclusions of one black olive, two sultanas and a slice of boiled egg. Whatever you land on, you’re unlikely to strike any duds.

Must order: The bestselling vanilla slice, with custard shimmering and quivering between crisp, light puff pastry.

title: Coracle
text:
These are our favourite spots for exceptional breakfast and brunch food, as featured in Good Food’s 100 Essential Melbourne Cafes and Bakeries 2026 guide. See all stories .

Vietnamese hot coffee blends condensed milk, evaporated milk and coconut milk with coffee that’s been dripped through a filter for eight hours.

8 / 8 Vietnamese hot coffee blends condensed milk, evaporated milk and coconut milk with coffee that’s been dripped through a filter for eight hours.

Coracle is perhaps best defined as a fine-dining cafe. There’s coffee and tea, eggs and toast, soup and sandwiches, but everything is rendered with deep thought, pristine ingredients and exquisite care. Just ask the toast: cloud-fluffy Japanese milk bread grilled over binchotan charcoal.

Owners Duong “Jane” Nu Duc Tam and her partner, Wong “Benny” Tat Ying are from Vietnam and Hong Kong respectively, and they’ve embraced a passion for Japanese culture and cuisine. Those three cultures are all represented on the menu, but the bedrock dish is the Japanese breakfast set, a tray of small dishes that includes fish, rice, soup, something fried, something grilled, a little sashimi and pickles.

The Viet coffee is its own feat of creativity: a blend of condensed, evaporated and coconut milks, steamed together with filter coffee. Breakfast really can be the most important meal of the day; Coracle reminds us to slow down and enjoy it.

Must order: The Japanese breakfast set. It’s a premium product and is $55 to account for that.

title: Perth’s cafes and bakeries have never been better – here’s 25 of the best
text:
Advertisement

Eating outEssential cafes and bakeries Perth’s cafes and bakeries have never been better – here’s 25 of the best In part one of the inaugural Essential Cafes and Bakeries guide, we celebrate the veteran venues and trailblazers that continue to blaze. Max Veenhuyzen July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

From the gym junkies to the surfers to the Crossfitters to the health-conscious accountants to the runners to the lycra cowboys to the FIFO workers that clog up airport passenger drop-offs at dawn, Perth folks are no strangers to an early alarm. Which means that cafes aren’t just part of life in P-Town: they’re a way of life. While there’s a utilitarian quality to the cafe genre, there’s room, like any hospitality venture, for excellence and craft too. Recognising such excellence and craft is the reason behind assembling Good Food’s inaugural Essential Perth Cafes and Bakeries guide: a roll call of 50 essential addresses that define Perth cafe and coffee culture circa 2026. To help everyone digest this hit list, I’ve split the guide into two 25-venue lists, each roughly broken into two main categories. List one drops today (July 24) and consists largely of the veteran venues and trailblazers that continue to blaze. The second list – a snapshot of the vanguard and the ones to watch – hits the streets next Friday (July 31). As well as being published on WAToday and across the Good Food network nationally, each venue will also be highlighted via the Good Food app. While an exercise like this is a fine excuse to drink heaps of batch brew, scoff pies and revisit old favourites, it also doubles as an opportunity to break down local cafe and coffee trends and consider how they help us understand this city, the people in it, and the effect(s) one has on the other. I’ve spent a lot of time studying the organic, single origin tea leaves and this is what they said.

Advertisement

Hinata’s brunch set. Jessica Shaver Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up Variety is the spiced latte of life If someone asked me to sum up Perth’s cafe landscape in a single word, I’d choose “diverse”. Compared to the cafe food doing the rounds at the start of the last decade – flat whites and cappuccinos, Eggs Benny and things with goat’s cheese, plus lots of square-shaped plates – modern-day brunching has become infinitely more cosmopolitan, interesting and delicious. Flavours from Japan, Malaysia, Vietnam and other Asian nations now represent a bigger slice of the short-order AM cook’s repertoire. Same goes for dishes and spices from North Africa and the Levant. (See also: “Middle Eastern cooking”) These global influences aren’t just being incorporated into the wider cafe cooking playbook but being celebrated in specialised new venues too. Consider cheery Japanesekissaten Hinata, or the Indonesian breakfast noodle shopBakmi Kochan 88. As a result, the definition of “cafe” has become bigger and looser to reflect the blurring of the lines between restaurant, bar, kiosk, takeaway counter, bakery, and about a dozen other styles of hospitality venue. (But in the interests of managing expectations, I define a cafe as a casual daytime-mostly restaurant or shop that serves breakfast, lunch or brunch, and predominantly coffee and tea.)

Advertisement

The noodles at Bakmi Kochan 88 are made daily - sometimes twice daily. Max Veenhuyzen The rise of craft bread, bakers and baked good Like the rest of the world, Perth has witnessed and benefited from the rise of specialistsandwich shops and bakeries over the past decade. The most interesting – places such as Tuart Hill’s glorious retro-leaningStrollio’s Luncheonette or the Highgate instituteChu Bakery for example – are a combination of both. Not that cafes necessarily have to bake their own sourdough, pastries and croissants, especially when one can call on makers such asMary Street Bakery,Big Loaf orCommon Bakery – wholesale bakeries that prove skill and scale can coexist – to supply and deliver them their daily bread. Chu Bakery in Highgate. Perth coffee is about more than long macs topped up

Advertisement

In addition to better baked goods for all, the rise of new Perth cafe empires – see alsoCommunity Coffee Co and Modus – has also raised coffee standards around town. (Most of the city’s new-wave coffee roasters are side hustles for existing cafes with the move to roasting their owns beans as much about quality control as it is tedious business talk trigger warning, vertical integration of supply chains and cost control.) While this growing local interest in specialty coffee should be applauded, it’s also worth noting that “specialty coffee” is also on the increase with operators using the s-word willy-nilly in a bid to catch customers’ eyes. (Understandable, perhaps, in such a competitive marketplace.) While there aren’t hard and fast rules on what constitutes specialty, it pays to have your wits about you: keep in mind the parable of the (alleged) “cool” uncle or auntie that constantly has to remind their nieces and nephews of their street-smarts. Modus Coffee in Victoria Park. (Speaking of the current cafe climate, it saddens me to see more places only serving coffee and hot drinks in takeaway cups. I get and appreciate that not paying someone to collect and wash cups helps owners manage wage costs, but it also seems wrong to watch somebody carefully whisk high-end matcha and then pour it into a paper cup. Remember to BYO cup.) Chu Bakery owners Ryan and Seren Chu.

Advertisement

Neighbourhood watch All this change plus the post-COVID shift to neighbourhood living has seen real cafe growth in suburbia. In the past, inner-city suburbs such as Mount Lawley, Nedlands, Subiaco and Northbridge plus the Perth and Fremantle CBDs dominated the good cafe and coffee discussion. Today, most postcodes boast at least one place to score a fried chicken burger, matcha latte or glazed doughnut. Although the majority of these places are pitched at the local community, some such as Willetton’s South African wonderlandKalahari or Wangara’s intriguing Burmese lunch barBlue Box are destination venues worth travelling for. Pastries at Common Bakery. Perth cafes are better and more interesting than ever

Advertisement

When it comes to cafes, West Australians have it good. It’s only when you do something as mildly insane as trying to distil Perth’s vibrant cafe and coffee culture into 50 essential addresses that you really understand just how good we have it, especially in the suburbs. This rising tide plus the nature of lists means that hard decisions have had to be made and that many worthy, deserving neighbourhood cafes didn’t make the cut. If one of those cafes happens to be your local, I say sorry. Please accept these recommendations as part of my apology. I have my fingers crossed that they’ll hip you to some new places you might want to add to your eating and drinking rotation. But more importantly, I hope this list captures the excitement and diversity of Perth’s casual morning food and drink scene. To all those that help this city rise and shine, day in and day out, we salute you. Thanks for making us the best morning versions of ourselves possible. All venues are reviewed anonymously. The best way to experience the guide is the Good Food app, where you can search via a map or collections. It’s available as a standalone subscription. Download it from the Apple App Store or the Google Play Store.

title: Get these four things right and you’ll have the perfect pie, every time
text:
Advertisement

Tips & adviceKnow how Get these four things right and you’ll have the perfect pie, every time Remember these four key pillars and your pie will linger in the memory long after the last flake of pastry falls off your shirt. Katrina Meynink July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Pie is a verb. Pie is a religion. A good pie inspires loyalty, heretics, even savage disputes. Mention soggy bottoms, hot water versus suet crusts, tomato sauce or no tomato sauce and watch otherwise level-headed adults lose their minds. These convictions often result in everyone knowing exactly what it takes to make a good pie. But like karaoke, this overstated confidence rarely reflects reality. Rather than let blind optimism fuel a mediocre pie, we need to stick to a few key principles. The anatomy of a great pie, be it sweet or savoury, is universal. Whether you’re making a slow-cooked beef pie (like this one, pictured below), a creamy chicken pie or something laden with fruit, our success comes down to getting four things right. Do this and even a humble mince pie starts punching well above its weight. Katrina’s ultimate beef and Guinness pie ticks all the pie pillar boxes. Katrina Meynink Pillar one: pastry A pie is only ever as good as its pastry. Pastry should leave flakes on your shirt. Strangely, pastry is often treated as little more than a container for the filling, when in reality it is responsible for half the eating experience. It should be crisp enough to hold its shape, flaky enough to shatter beneath your fork and sturdy enough to support the filling without buckling. And the key to pie architecture success: using two types of pastry.

Advertisement

According to Caitlin Gallagher, owner of Jocelyn’s Provisions in Brisbane, the perfect pie is all about the balance of the best ingredients and pastry textures. “Start with an indulgent velvety sour cream pastry base, then for balance top with a light and flaky butter puff pastry with layers that offer crisp and crunch.” The best recipes from Australia's leading chefs straight to your inbox. Sign up Think about your favourite pie. Chances are it isn’t memorable because everything tastes the same. It’s memorable because there’s a surprise every few bites. Then comes the mistake that undoes all that good work: hot filling, aka the fastest route to a soggy base that will buckle as you lift that pie from plate to mouth. Allow fillings to cool before assembling. To further ensure a firm base, particularly with a double crust pie, always brush the inside with an egg wash and blind bake the base until lightly golden. Doing this before adding any filling helps to create a moisture barrier. Katrina’s haloumi spanakopita: Sure to leave pastry flakes on your shirt. Katrina Meynink

Advertisement

Pillar two: seasoning Pastry is buttery, rich and relatively mild. It’s not usually pre-seasoned, meaning the filling has to do the heavy lifting. One of the biggest mistakes home cooks make is seasoning their filling until it tastes “just right” in the saucepan. Once wrapped in pastry, those flavours become muted. The butter (the fat) in the pastry works to round out flavours and chefs often recommend over-seasoning the filling to compensate. A cross-section of a lovingly made pie. Katrina Meynink According to chef Marty Boetz, of Brisbane’s Shortgrain, adding a thickener like corn flour or a basic slurry to thicken the sauce of your filling also really impacts seasoning.

Advertisement

“A thickener softens flavour, so it’s important to taste repeatedly as you cook. Don’t be afraid of it. Use your salt. Also think about sweetness and acidity. A splash of Worcestershire sauce, fish sauce, vinegar, mustard or lemon juice can brighten a rich filling and help carry flavour so that your pie is expertly seasoned. You will be much happier with the end result.” We also shouldn’t forget the role of herbs. Always add at the end for freshness, rather than cooking away their flavour. This will also help add depth and seasoning to your pie. The goal isn’t to make the filling salty or sour. It’s to make it taste like the very best version of itself. Marty Boetz and Jocelyn Provisions’ yellow curry chicken and pumpkin pie. Pillar three: filling If you’ve ever cut into a pie only to watch the filling spill across the plate, you already know why consistency matters. There needs to be moisture without sloppiness, structure without bulging insides.

Advertisement

The best fillings don’t simply taste good; they give your mouth something to do. It should hold together just enough to stay inside the slice while remaining luscious and spoonable. The trick isn’t simply adding more flour or corn flour, it’s about reducing sauces properly before they reach the pastry. Slow cooking naturally concentrates flavour, while gelatine from meat helps create body. Cream-based fillings benefit from cooling before assembling, giving the sauce time to thicken. Think about your favourite pie. Chances are it isn’t memorable because everything tastes the same. It’s memorable because there’s a surprise every few bites. Your filling is an opportunity to create structure and contrast. Sometimes it’s just one unexpected ingredient stopping the whole thing from becoming beige. Tender braised beef loves the sweetness of caramelised onions. Creamy chicken benefits from bursts of peas. Even fruit pies rely on contrast, pairing soft fruit with crunchy sugar-crusted pastry. Sugar crunch pastry: Katrina’s apple and Earl Grey pie. Katrina Meynink

Advertisement

Pillar four: The finish Great pies are elevated by dozens of tiny decisions and the final flourish often transforms a pie from good to memorable. An egg wash should leave pastry deeply golden rather than beige. A scattering of flaky salt catches on buttery pastry layers, adding crunch and little bursts of savouriness. Spices and seeds can be used to hint at what’s inside, while a dusting of sugar on a fruit pie creates a brittle, caramelised crust that cracks satisfyingly under your fork. Steam vents aren’t just decorative; they’re insurance against a soggy lid, but even they can become part of the pie’s personality when cut into leaves, stars or neat little slashes. Katrina’s mushroom stroganoff pot pie. Katrina Meynink Then there are the accompaniments. A spoonful of sharp pickle beside a beef pie. A slick of bright green salsa verde cutting through slow-cooked lamb. Vanilla ice cream surrendering to a still-warm fruit pie. Good pie makers know the finish doesn’t stop when the pie comes out of the oven, it extends to everything that makes you reach immediately for another bite.

Advertisement

Perhaps that’s why pie lovers are so opinionated. Great pies are built on tiny decisions, each one almost invisible on its own, but together they are the difference between a pie you’d politely eat and one you’d happily drive across town for. Before long you’re brushing pastry flakes off your shirt and deciding if a second pie counts as poor judgement or an excellent life decision. title: Yellow curry chicken and pumpkin pies
text:
Step 1 Preheat the oven to 180C fan-forced (200C conventional).

Step 2 To make the yellow curry paste, dry-roast the onion, garlic, coriander roots, turmeric and ginger in a small heavy-based saucepan or wok over medium heat, stirring frequently until coloured and soft, about 8-10 minutes. You want them to caramelise to give the finished curry flavour depth. Scrape into a large bowl with the chillies. Set aside to cool before transferring to a food processor and blending for 3-4 minutes to a smooth reddish-yellow paste. Add water, a tablespoon at a time, if the paste is too dry.

Step 3 To make the spice mix, dry-roast the spices slowly in a small heavy-based saucepan or wok over medium heat until toasted and fragrant. When cool, grind with the peppercorns and salt in a spice or coffee grinder. Stir the spice mix into the paste and mix thoroughly to combine.

Step 4 To poach the chicken, add coconut milk, stock, makrut leaves, fish sauce, oyster sauce and palm sugar to a large saucepan. Bring to a simmer. Taste and season, using sugar and fish sauce as required to balance. Bring to a boil. Add the chicken, then reduce heat to low and gently simmer until the chicken is just cooked through.

Step 5 In a separate large high-sided pan, cook the paste and coconut oil until fragrant. Transfer chicken and poaching liquid to the paste. Simmer, stirring regularly, until the mixture begins to thicken and has reduced by at least a quarter. Stir through the cornflour slurry until fully incorporated and the sauce thickens and generously coats the back of a spoon. Add the roasted pumpkin and stir gently.

Step 6 Grease six 150ml pie tins. Cut six 15cm rounds from shortcrust pastry and six 12cm rounds from puff pastry. Press the shortcrust rounds into pie tins and trim the excess pastry. Divide the chicken mixture between pie tins, brush the edges with the egg wash, then top with puff pastry rounds, pressing the edges together firmly. Brush the top of the pies with egg wash, cut small slits in tops and scatter with salt flakes. Refrigerate for 15 minutes, then bake until golden and pastry is cooked (about 40 minutes).

Step 7 Meanwhile, make the cucumber quick pickle. To make the pickling liquid, combine the rice vinegar, sugar, water and a pinch of salt in a small saucepan and bring to a boil. Remove from the heat when the sugar dissolves and allow to cool. It should taste both sour and sweet.

Step 8 Place the ginger, finely sliced eschalots, and thinly sliced red chilli in a small bowl. Leaving the skin on the cucumber, slice lengthways into quarters. Remove the seeds from the centre. Slice the cucumber at an angle into 5cm dice and mix with the julienned ginger and sliced eschalots and chillies.

title: Brisbane’s cafes and bakeries aren’t what they used to be. They’re better
text:
Advertisement

Eating outFood Brisbane’s cafes and bakeries aren’t what they used to be. They’re better Brisbane’s brunch scene has changed a lot over the past decade. We celebrate the best operators in the inaugural Essential Cafes and Bakeries of Brisbane guide. Matt Shea July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

It’s easy to think it started during the pandemic. Brisbane was shut down. Restaurants and cafes couldn’t operate. So they adapted – fast. For cafes, that meant turning themselves into a coffee window or a bakery or a specialised grocer – whatever it was, as long as it was grab-and-go. Anything to keep some sort of cashflow ticking over. Idle in New Farm. Markus Ravik But adversity has a habit of creating opportunity. Take Pawpaw in East Brisbane, back then still the buzzy engine of the small fiefdom of venues owned and operated by Giorgina Venzin. Venzin turned it into a pop-up bakery with the help of baker Lindsay Krahenbring, who’d found herself out of work as a pastry chef after the shutdown of Hillstone, St Lucia Golf Links’ wedding and functions venue.

Advertisement

Related Article Brisbane’s best cafes and bakeries (on the northside) in 2026 Krahenbring was (and remains) a weapon, Pawpaw’s bakery pop-up turned into a hit, and within a year, Venzin had created a bakery, Darvella, in Bulimba with Krahenbring at the helm. Darvella is now regarded as up there among Brisbane’s best bakeries. Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up The pandemic was full of these sorts of stories: things becoming other things, and then creating other things again. Salud cafe & restaurant in Auchenflower. But you could argue COVID just accelerated what was already happening in Brisbane’s cafe scene by early 2020. Diners were becoming more sophisticated, so the classic full-service, order-at-the-table cafe with a broad menu and well-made espresso wasn’t quite what they were chasing anymore. Not all the time, anyway.

Advertisement

They wanted pour over coffee and batch brew. They wanted ceremonial-grade matcha. Eggs Benedict was fine, sure, but why not omurice or fancy congee or egg drop sandwiches? Pre-made cabinet sandwiches became showstopping chef-made numbers, bakeries began to drill down on kouign-amanns or eclairs or custard tarts. Queen Amann’s specialty? Kouign-amann, naturally. Markus Ravik As for operators, reducing a venue down to a focused offering means better control of overheads in an age when the cost of everything seems to be going up. The result in 2026 is the Brisbane cafe scene has never been more diverse. Breakfast and brunch look a lot different in today’s collection of 30 reviews that make up Part 1 of the inaugural Essential Brisbane Cafes and Bakeries guide of 2026. We live in an age of venues doing one or two things and doing them well, whether it be coffee, matcha, baked sweets or toasties. And it’s in all corners of town.

Advertisement

Sarni’s chef-made sandwiches are some of the best in town. Markus Ravik Mountain River Patisserie has become a mecca for creative pastries and onigiri in Runcorn, Sarni makes some of Brisbane’s best sandwiches in Ascot, and Passport is celebrated for its speciality coffee in Northgate. Don’t get us wrong, there are still great cafes in the traditional sense of the word, but they’re fewer and further between, and often need to do be exceptional to cut through: think Kin in Teneriffe, or Goodfolk in Bardon, or Florence in Camp Hill. Florence sits on a sleepy suburban corner in bucolic Camp Hill. The other trend we’ve noticed recently? The return of the all-day venue – the kind of place that feels right whether it’s serving you brunch, lunch or dinner. Emme is perhaps the most prominent example right now – Tommy Tze Lian’s slick James Street diner easily switching from heaving brunch services to moody dinners – but over the road The Green pulls a similar trick on weekends and, up on Arthur Street, Bistro Tino will do the same come September. Running a hospitality business in 2026 is hard. After years of pandemics and inflation and interest rate fluctuations and natural disasters and war, many operators, if given a chance, will wonder aloud to you why they do it. And that’s a bummer because what they’re doing has never, ever been better.

title: ‘It tastes phenomenal’: Southside newcomer serves gnocchi, but not as you might know it
text:
Advertisement

Review Eating outSouth Yarra ‘It tastes phenomenal’: Southside newcomer serves gnocchi, but not as you might know it Mudo weaves cornerstone Korean ingredients – gochujang, buckwheat, soju, sesame – into the types of dishes Melbourne diners would recognise from any inner-city restaurant menu. Emma Breheny July 24, 2026 Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

1 / 8 Gnocchi with gochujang-infused burnt butter. Bonnie Savage 2 / 8 Potato rosti with salted cod roe, creme fraiche, mackerel and caviar. Bonnie Savage 3 / 8 Mudo’s wagyu tartare, seasoned with shallots, chives, soy sauce and sesame oil, is mixed with diced nashi pear. Bonnie Savage 4 / 8 Mudo is housed in the former Atlas Dining site. Bonnie Savage 5 / 8 Duck breast with pumpkin, beetroot, soy plum sauce and kohlrabi kimchi. Bonnie Savage 6 / 8 Nori makguksu - buckwheat noodles with nori vinaigrette and kale. Bonnie Savage 7 / 8 Me-mil - buckwheat cake, doenjang caramel, toasted nuts and seeds, gochujang tuille and sesame ice-cream. Bonnie Savage 8 / 8 The dessert incorporates all three of Korea’s “mother sauces”. Bonnie Savage Previous Slide Next Slide 14 / 20 How we score Mudo Dining & Bar Korean$$$$ Potato gnocchi in a red sauce is placed on the table. Golden from the pan, soft enough to cut with a spoon, the long rectangles of pasta are sprinkled with parmesan. But this isn’t an Italian restaurant. The sauce is stained by gochujang, the earthy Korean chilli paste known for its firepower, except here it’s tamed by browned butter. Mudo chef Happy Cho created this dish with one eye trained on Korea’s chewy oblong-shaped rice cakes – tteokbokki – and the other on his typical customer, more likely to know their gnocchi from their rigatoni. Like a Harry Potter novel, the dish says different things to different groups. The main thing, though, is that it tastes phenomenal. Mudo is housed in the former Atlas Dining site. Bonnie Savage

Advertisement

Cho opened Mudo quietly before Christmas with front-of-house manager Whale Kim and a motto: Aussie vibes, Korean edge. The long dining room is tastefully dressed in creams and caramels. The food, though, is anything but beige. Cornerstone Korean ingredients – gochujang, buckwheat, soju, sesame – are woven into the types of dishes diners would recognise from any inner-city restaurant menu of the past three years. Restaurant reviews, news and the hottest openings served to your inbox. Sign up The Korean-born Cho has cooked everywhere from sushi restaurants to the finest of fine diners over two decades. Interning at San Francisco’s Benu in 2019, he saw Korean-American chef Corey Lee weave his culture into menus that scooped up awards. Could Cho also draw on his different experiences as a chef and create something unique? Wagyu tartare features diced nashi pear. Bonnie Savage His steak tartare walks the line between wine-bar favourite and yukhoe, Korea’s dish of raw beef. Wagyu seasoned with shallots, chives, soy sauce and sesame oil is mixed with diced nashi pear, a favourite tenderising ingredient for Korean chefs.

Advertisement

Rich, bronze-skinned duck shares the plate with pumpkin and beetroot, but also lozenges of kohlrabi turned into electrifying kimchi. Take one and consult your doctor if symptoms persist. Buckwheat, grown in Korea far longer than wheat, is as important to the cuisine as butter is to France or masalas are to India. It’s used here in noodles that play counterweight to Mudo’s bigger flavours, and in a sponge cake, torn over salted caramel with sesame ice-cream and a tuile wafer that’s sweet, salty and spicy. Some tables are trying their first ssam, the lettuce wraps that add crunch and contrast to Korean barbecued meat. Others need no introduction to jangajji, the pickles that brighten a barramundi fish burger, or baekseju, the herbal rice wine that replaces Campari in the house negroni. You can ask about unfamiliar ingredients and staff will help, but there were so few waitstaff across my visits that you’re unlikely to get a spiel when you first open your menu. Some people will love that, but in this case, there’s lots to say. Me-mil - buckwheat cake, doenjang caramel, toasted nuts and seeds, gochujang tuille and sesame ice-cream. Bonnie Savage

Advertisement

That sponge cake dish? It incorporates all three of Korea’s “mother sauces”, I later find out. Doenjang, a soybean paste similar to miso, deepens the caramel. Gochujang brings the heat in the wafer. And ganjang, Korean soy sauce, adds brittle crunch to toasted nuts and seeds. Other dishes are there simply because they’re delicious. A neat golden brick of prawn toast is never a bad idea. Potato rosti topped with torched mackerel is two godly bites of fish-and-potato, a timeless combo. Dishes are priced at what can only be called a steal in 2026. A main course of duck for less than $55. A proper, entree-sized plate of gnocchi for $18. Half a chicken with carrot and pickled fennel: $29. People are rightly sensitive about prices, but I wonder if charging a few extra dollars across the menu would pay for another waiter on the floor, and result in a better experience all round. On the way home, I see a billboard advertising Subway’s latest special. It’s a steak sandwich with Korean barbecue sauce. Aussie vibes, Korean edge? The low-down Atmosphere: Softly lit room adds a backdrop to thrills on the plate Go-to dishes: Wagyu tartare ($24); gnocchi with gochujang ($18); duck breast ($42); nori makguksu noodles ($12); me-mil buckwheat cake ($14) Drinks: Cocktails starring Korean rice wine (soju); considered non-alcoholics; affordable Australian, Italian and French wines Cost: About $140 for two, excluding drinks

Advertisement

This review was originally published in Good Weekend magazine. Good Food reviews are booked anonymously and paid independently. A restaurant can’t pay for a review or inclusion in the Good Food Guide.

title: Nathan Cleary drops huge update on playing future
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLNRL 2026 Nathan Cleary drops huge update on playing future Christian Nicolussi Updated July 24, 2026 — 12:00pm ,first published July 24, 2026 — 5:00am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Panthers champion Nathan Cleary has provided a huge update on his playing future, all but ruling out a potential move to the New Zealand Warriors because of fears he could block the path of his little brother, Jett. What Cleary does beyond the 2027 season – and even if he is prepared to head to the open market on November 1 – has become one of rugby league’s biggest storylines. Class act: Nathan Cleary checks in on Tallyn Da Silva. NRL The Warriors, where Cleary’s father Ivan coached and played, has been one rival club regularly linked to the halfback, along with the cashed-up PNG Chiefs and their tax exemptions. Cleary said he had used the past few weeks to settle back into club life at Penrith after leading NSW to a memorable Origin series win, and was no certainty to become a free agent.

Advertisement

The Panthers remain hopeful of getting a deal done, and even sitting down with him and his management in the coming weeks. As for a move to the Warriors, which was floated this week in News Corp, Cleary said: “I grew up [in Auckland] a fair [few years], and I will admit I was a Warriors fan growing up. “But at the end of the day, even if that was an option [to play for them], I’d be blocking my little brother’s pathway. I’m not too sure about [doing] that. “I want to put a bit more thought into it. The last few weeks there have been a bit of a comedown from Origin, and I’ve been trying to get back into a routine with the boys, and get back into consistent training. “That sort of [contract] stuff will work itself out in the background.”

Advertisement

Jett, 21, was in the Penrith system before heading to the Warriors, where he is yet to make his NRL debut. Nathan was glad the Panthers could get the 24-18 win over western Sydney rivals Parramatta on Thursday night, but felt bad one of his kicks slammed into Tallyn Da Silva’s head from close range, forcing him off the field for a head injury assessment, which he later passed. Nathan Cleary’s kick didn’t miss Tallyn Da Silva. Nine There was a nice touch when Cleary made a point of checking in on da Silva as he rolled around in pain while being helped by a trainer in the middle of CommBank Stadium. “I would not like that happening to myself; I wanted to see if he was all right [because] it cranked his neck a bit too,” Cleary said.

Advertisement

“I remember we had a captain’s run, I was practising 40-20s, [former Panther] Api Koroisau was just in front of me, and I did that exact same thing. He was wearing an earring, and the earring popped out. “It was the day before a game, and he was lucky he didn’t get concussed. It was so similar to [the] one tonight.” Da Silva said he would be fine to play next Sunday against his former club the Wests Tigers in what will double as his 50th NRL game. “It felt like a rocket,” Da Silva said of the Cleary cannon. “It hit me in the back of my head, my ears started ringing, and my head was stinging. It really hurt in the moment. It was partially my fault because I turned around and clocked off for a bit.

Advertisement

“Once I stood up and walked around it went away. It’s bound to happen every now and then. I was just unlucky.” The Panthers push on to Mudgee next weekend where they play Canberra. Fullback Dylan Edwards missed the Eels clash because of a shoulder injury – not because of the 10 stitches required for a nasty head gash last week – and is no certainty to return against the Raiders. His replacement, Jaxen Edgar, was excellent in his second NRL game. Winger Tom Jenkins took his season tally to 26 tries, which broke Rhys Wesser’s long-standing club record of 25 set in 2003 – the year Penrith won the premiership. title: Real victims of new Trump tariff revealed
text:
US President Donald Trump has hit Australia with a new 12.5 per cent tariff on imports, but Americans will ultimately be asked to pick up the bill.

The Albanese government, which had lobbied for months against any increase of tariffs on Australia, has criticised the decision as "completely unjustified".

Trade Minister Don Farrell said the government was disappointed but not surprised by the White House announcement on Friday morning AEST.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

It said new tariffs of between 10 and 12.5 per cent would be imposed on 60 trading ‌partners from midnight US time on Friday (2pm AEST).

The US said the new tariffs were in response to trading partners failing to act against forced labour.

US President Donald Trump has imposed a 12.5 per cent tariff on Australian imports to the US. Picture: Saul Loeb/Pool/AFP

"We believe that among all of the countries in the world, Australia does take the issue of slavery, modern slavery seriously, and will continue to do that," Senator Farrell told reporters in Adelaide.

"But the Albanese government believes that this imposition of higher tariffs on Australia is completely unjustified, and we will continue to lobby the United States trade representative to remove all tariffs on Australian goods."

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Senator Farrell said there would be some carve outs in the new tariffs, including beef, gold and copper.

"The 67 per cent increase in trade has been because the value of those products, and the volume of those products, has been increasing," he said.

"I don't see any reason to think that that's not going to continue."

Real victims of tariffs

Independent economist Saul Eslake said Australian businesses wouldn't pay more due to the tariffs.

"The primary casualty of these new tariffs are American consumers who will be paying higher prices for things imported from Australia with the exception of beef," Mr Eslake told Sky News.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Last year Mr Trump was forced to concede that the tariffs he imposed on the beef industry including Australia was driving up meat prices in the US.

But Mr Eslake noted Australian exporters could be impacted if they face higher tariffs compared with other countries.

He also questioned whether the US would keep other trade and security deals including ANZUS and AUKUS.

"This is a flagrant breach of the Australian US free trade agreement that John Howard and George W Bush signed a little over 20 years ago," he said.

"It does raise the question, if we can't trust the Americans to abide by the terms of the free trade agreement they have with us, can we trust them to abide by the terms of any other agreement we have them such as ANZUS or AUKUS treaty."

No reason for decision

Australian business leaders have weighed in on the decision to hit Australian exports with the new tariff.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Business Council of Australia chief executive Bran Black called it a disappointing decision that penalised Australian businesses for no justifiable reason.

"This decision will make it harder for Australian businesses to compete and sell their products into the US market, hurting investment and our jobs," Mr Black said.

"Australia already runs a trade deficit with the United States, and we remain deeply integrated partners. The US is our largest two-way investment partner, with total investment exceeding $2 trillion."

Business Council of Australia chief executive Bran Black says there is no justification for the US tariffs. . Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman.

Mr Black said he would work with the Australian government and Washington officials to minimise the impacts on local businesses and overturn the ban.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"The United States Trade Representative's finding rests on the narrow claim that Australia lacks a US-style ban on importing goods made with forced labour despite our already strong Modern Slavery Act and without identifying a single company, enforcement failure or quantified trade impact," he said.

Makes no sense

Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles says the US decision makes no sense as Australia has "some of the strongest laws in the world" to combat the entry of goods made with forced labour.

"We obviously are supportive of an open global trading system. That's actually what has seen an enormous rise in productivity and prosperity around the world," he told ABC Radio National shortly after the announcement.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"In relation to our trading relationship with the United States, the US Australia-US Free Trade Agreement has been really important in terms of promoting jobs in both countries and providing for freer trade and lower prices between both countries."

Australia and Anthony Albanese have protested against Trump tariffs, calling them unwarranted.

In a formal submission made earlier in July, Australia reiterated its opposition to Mr Trump's tariffs, arguing Australia had some of the world's strongest legislation to tackle forced labour. .

Mr Marles said Australia would continue to lobby against the tariffs.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has previously argued against US tariffs, calling them unwarranted. Picture: NewsWire / Brenton Edwards

US Trade Ambassador Jamieson Greer said the decision was "correct", accusing countries of failing to act on forced labour.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"President Trump recognises that decades of moral suasion have not eradicated forced labour from global supply chains," Ambassador Greer said.

"The United States has had a forced labour import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it.

"It's well past time for our trading partners to do the same.

"Today's action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere.

"I am encouraged by the trading partners who have moved quickly to adopt forced labour import prohibitions, and look forward to ensuring their effective enforcement."

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

The new tariff come as the 10 per cent tariff was due to expire.

The temporary 150 day 10 per cent tariffs were implemented as a stopgap measure after the Supreme Court ruled in February that the sweeping duties imposed by President Trump under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act were illegal.

The new tariffs have been imposed under Section 301 of the Trade Act, which allows the US to combat unfair trade practices by other countries, rather than the sweeping presidential emergency powers Trump used for his earlier "reciprocal" tariffs.

title: Aussie tradie exposes dodgy practice after elderly woman hit with $10,000 quote: 'Happens a lot'
text:
An Aussie plumber has hit out at dodgy tradies "taking advantage" of vulnerable people, with the elderly in particular often being overcharged by thousands.

Andrew David, director at About Plumbing Time Sydney, said he recently received a call from a man about low water pressure at his elderly mother's home in southwest Sydney.

The experienced plumber said he was shocked to learn they'd previously been quoted $10,000 for what he said was a simple job.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"The client's son contacted me regarding a low water pressure issue. When I called [them] to book the job in, he let me know that they were quoted $10,000," Andrew told Yahoo News.

He explained it was a simple fix that any plumber could tackle easily.

"The flow restrictors in some of the taps and the showers were all blocked up, so I took them all out and the pressure increased," he said.

He also replaced the pressure valve on the water metre as requested, because "it was quite old".

"From a $10,000 quote, it was definitely under $1,000 for what we did," Andrew revealed.

Do you have a story about ? Contact

The plumber also replaced the pressure valve on the water meter as requested, and still the total cost was far cheaper. (About Plumbing Time Sydney)

Why do some tradies overcharge for jobs?

According to the plumber, who has 17 years' experience, the previous tradie claimed he had to "cut sections of the wall and replace the pipework".

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

In Andrew's opinion, this wasn't necessary and was likely suggested to overcharge for the job.

He said it's a common tactic used by dodgy tradies, often "targeting the elderly".

RELATED:

They charge extortionate amounts for small items or create unnecessary damage to justify higher invoices.

"It happens a lot," the plumber said, adding it's an issue he sees every week.

Particularly among larger maintenance companies, he said, which are usually "commission-based", meaning the higher they charge, the more money in their pocket.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"It gets the plumbing [industry] fired up when they see quotes like this," he said.

"It's sad to see there are individuals taking advantage of people, but luckily, our client wanted a second opinion."

Homeowners are warned to shop around and always seek a second quote. (Getty Images)

His advice to Aussies is to always shop around.

"Don't settle for the first quote," he said.

"It's not bad to get a second opinion and compare the prices that way. If they're pushy or come across like a real salesman, seek another quote."

What does the law say about trades' quotes?

Previously, lawyers Alison and Jillian Barrett from Maurice Blackburn, told Yahoo News, tradies, generally, can charge however they see fit, so long as it's deemed "reasonable".

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"In Australia, there is no fixed hourly rate or legal cap on what plumbers or other trades can charge for their services," they said.

"Trades are generally free to set their own prices based on their business model, overheads, expertise, and market demand."

title: ABC pulls sacked journo’s 4 Corners stories
text:
Two episodes of the ABC's flagship investigative program produced by a now-sacked journalist with underworld links have been taken down over accuracy concerns.

The national broadcaster has pulled Meth Highway (2024) and Cocaine Nation (2023) – two episodes of Four Corners spearheaded by sacked ABC journo Mahmood Fazal.

Sacked reporter Mahmood Fazal was the journalist behind both episodes. Picture: NewsWire/ Gaye Gerard

A statement from ABC management said the programs had been removed from the broadcaster's digital platforms "following concerns identified about the veracity of certain sources used in both programs".

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"The matter has been referred to the ABC ombudsman for an independent review of whether the programs complied with the relevant editorial standards as set out in the editorial policies," the statement reads.

"The ABC is committed to the highest standards of editorial integrity.

"Maintaining the trust of Australians depends on upholding those standards, and we take any concerns about our journalism seriously."

Fazal was sacked by the ABC from his role as an investigative reporter in 2026 after an investigation was launched into his appearance on a podcast led by a former underworld figure.

Mahmood Fazal is a Walkley Award-winning journalist and ex-Mongol bikie gang member. Picture: Instagram

ABC managing director Hugh Marks was grilled about Fazal by Liberal senator Sarah Henderson during his appearance at a senate estimates hearing in May.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

"We conducted an investigation into matters that were raised about Mr Fazal in relation to an external work request that me made to the ABC," Mr Marks said.

"That investigation has now been completed … and as a result of that investigation, Mr Fazal is no longer employed by the ABC."

Mr Marks said Fazal "was terminated", but it would be "inappropriate" for him to go into details on the investigation, which he said Fazal had participated in.

Four Corners is one of the country's most celebrated investigative news programs. Picture: ABC

Senator Henderson put to Mr Marks that details should be released, as she believed "Four Corners has fallen well short of the standards that should apply".

ABC's editorial director Gavin Fang was also asked about whether Fazal should have been hired due to his past criminal associations.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

He said the former bikie joined as an award-winning journalist who brought "a number of different types of stories that were important to tell to the public, not withstanding any of the allegations you've outlined there".

Fazal was sacked by the ABC earlier this year. Picture: Instagram

Mr Fang said Fazal had been upfront about his past organised crime connections, which "allowed him to tell stories about what was going on in the criminal underworld".

Senator Henderson hit back, saying if Fazal had "a good story to tell, it doesn't mean you've got to hire him as a journalist".

It is understood Fazal had worked on a podcast called Word on the Street in conjunction with independent journalist Ryan Naumenko, but the pair had a falling out regarding payment.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Fazal is a Walkley Award-winning journalist and a former Mongols outlaw motorcycle club member.

His unique history granted him access to contacts from across Australia's criminal underworld, and his work has appeared in several high-profile national publications.
